tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: FLOT (Fluorouracil, Leucovorin, Oxaliplatin, Docetaxel) – Perioperative

trial_data.notes: Compared to ECF (epirubicin, cisplatin, fluorouracil) in patients with resectable non-metastatic gastric or EGJ adenocarcinoma (≥cT2 and/or N+). FLOT was associated with higher pCR (16% vs 6%; P=0.02) and significant OS improvement. Grade 3–4 adverse events: 25% vs 40% in phase II part. ECF should no longer be recommended.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Perioperative systemic therapy is preferred for patients with resectable esophageal or EGJ adenocarcinoma who are medically fit with access to frequent toxicity evaluation. 4 cycles preoperative and 4 cycles postoperative. Reference: [168, 293]

trial_data.overall_survival: Median OS 49 months vs 24 months (HR 0.657; 95% CI 0.495–0.871; P=0.003)
trial_data.notes: Preoperative chemoradiation with paclitaxel/carboplatin + RT (41.4 Gy) vs surgery alone. Improved OS and R0 resection rate (92% vs 69%). pCR higher in SCC (49%) vs adenocarcinoma (23%). Long-term follow-up: median OS 48.6 months vs 24 months (HR 0.68; 95% CI 0.53–0.88; P=0.003). References: [167, 310]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Esophageal and esophagogastric junction (EGJ) cancers are malignant neoplasms arising from the mucosa of the esophagus, a muscular tube derived from the foregut that extends from the cricopharyngeus (approximately 15 cm from the incisors) to the gastroesophageal junction. The primary histologic types are squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and adenocarcinoma, which differ in etiology, pathology, tumor location, prognosis, and treatment approach [9]. SCC typically arises in the upper and middle thoracic esophagus, often associated with direct carcinogen exposure from tobacco and alcohol. Adenocarcinoma most commonly occurs in the distal esophagus and EGJ, frequently in the setting of Barrett esophagus (BE), a metaplastic condition where the normal squamous epithelium is replaced by columnar epithelium predisposed to malignancy [21]. The 8th edition of the AJCC staging manual defines tumors with epicenter ≤2 cm into the proximal stomach as esophageal carcinomas, creating a unified framework for EGJ cancers [10]. Cervical esophageal tumors (<5 cm from the cricopharyngeus) are managed as a distinct entity with definitive chemoradiation [ESOPH-C 1 of 3]. Clinical staging incorporates endoscopic resection (ER) for early tumors, endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), CT, and FDG-PET/CT to determine resectability and guide multidisciplinary management [ESOPH-1].

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
subtypes.description: Malignancy arising from the squamous epithelium of the esophagus, most commonly localized at or above the tracheal bifurcation. Has a proclivity for earlier lymphatic spread and is associated with a poorer prognosis compared to adenocarcinoma [9,10].
subtypes.frequency: SCC accounts for less than 30% of all esophageal cancers in the United States and Western Europe, but remains the most common histology in Eastern Europe, Asia, and the 'esophageal cancer belt' spanning from northern Iran through Central Asia into Northern China [1,5].
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with tobacco and alcohol consumption [11-13]. Risk decreases substantially after smoking cessation [14,15]. Hereditary predisposition includes tylosis with esophageal cancer (TEC) due to RHBDF2 germline mutations, Bloom syndrome, and Fanconi anemia [26-28,40,43,44].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Malignancy arising from columnar or glandular epithelium, most often in the distal esophagus and EGJ. The incidence has risen sharply in North America and Western Europe, reflecting increasing rates of obesity [1].
subtypes.frequency: Adenocarcinoma is now the dominant histologic type in the United States, accounting for over 70% of esophageal cancers. It is more common in non-Hispanic white males [7,8].
subtypes.key_associations: Key risk factors include chronic GERD, Barrett esophagus (30–60 times increased risk), obesity, male sex, age >50 years, and tobacco use (moderate risk) [12,16-20,22-24]. HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression occurs in 15–30% of adenocarcinomas [150-153]. Familial Barrett esophagus (FBE) suggests inherited susceptibility [31-33].

subtypes.name: Mixed Adenosquamous Carcinoma and Other Rare Subtypes
subtypes.description: Includes tumors with both squamous and glandular differentiation, as well as carcinomas not otherwise classified. These are staged using the TNM system for SCC [ESOPH-B 1 of 7].
subtypes.frequency: Extremely rare; exact incidence not specified in the source.
subtypes.key_associations: No specific risk factors beyond those for SCC and adenocarcinoma.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Esophageal carcinogenesis involves the accumulation of genetic aberrations, including overexpression of growth factors/receptors, alterations in DNA damage response, and loss of genomic stability [Discussion MS-9]. In SCC, common alterations include TP53 mutations, copy number gains in CCND1 and EGFR, and frequent loss of heterozygosity at multiple loci; the disease is molecularly distinct from adenocarcinoma [Discussion MS-2]. For adenocarcinoma, the molecular landscape shows recurrent amplifications of ERBB2 (HER2) in 15–30% of cases, ERBB2 amplifications are more frequent in EGJ than gastric tumors [150-157]. HER2 positivity is assessed by IHC (scores 0–3+) with confirmatory FISH/ISH for equivocal cases [ESOPH-B 3 of 7]. Microsatellite instability (MSI) due to mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) occurs in a subset of gastroesophageal adenocarcinomas and is tested universally by PCR, NGS, or IHC [ESOPH-B 5 of 7]. PD-L1 expression measured by combined positive score (CPS) or tumor area positivity (TAP) is used to identify patients likely to benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors [ESOPH-B 5 of 7]. Rare targetable fusions include NTRK1/2/3 (TRK), RET gene fusions, and BRAF V600E mutations [ESOPH-B 6 of 7]. CLDN18.2 expression, assessed by IHC (≥75% viable tumor cells with moderate-to-strong staining), defines a subset of adenocarcinomas amenable to zolbetuximab therapy [ESOPH-B 4 of 7]. The molecular heterogeneity underscores the need for comprehensive biomarker testing (IHC/ISH/targeted PCR followed by NGS) to guide targeted therapy [ESOPH-B 6 of 7].

epidemiology.incidence: In 2020, there were an estimated 604,000 new cases of esophageal cancer globally, making it the seventh most frequently diagnosed cancer worldwide [2,3]. The age-standardized incidence varies 60-fold between high- and low-incidence regions, with the highest rates in the 'esophageal cancer belt' from northern Iran through Central Asia to northern China [4]. In the United States, approximately 21,560 new cases were expected in 2023 [8]. Adenocarcinoma incidence has been rising in North America and Western Europe, while SCC remains predominant in Eastern Europe and Asia [1].
epidemiology.mortality: Esophageal cancer caused more than 544,000 deaths in 2020, ranking as the sixth leading cause of cancer death globally [2,3]. In the United States, it is the eleventh leading cause of cancer death, with an estimated 16,120 deaths in 2023 [8]. The 5-year survival rate remains low (<20% for all stages combined) [8].
epidemiology.trends: SCC incidence has declined in North America and Western Europe due to reduced tobacco and alcohol use, now representing <30% of cases in the US [1]. Adenocarcinoma incidence has increased sharply, likely driven by rising obesity rates, GERD prevalence, and Barrett esophagus [1,12,16-18].
epidemiology.demographics: Adenocarcinoma predominantly affects non-Hispanic white males, with risk increasing after age 50. SCC is more common in males and in populations with high tobacco/alcohol use, and in certain racial/ethnic groups, including African Americans in the US and individuals in high-incidence regions of Asia and Africa [7,8,22-24].

risk_factors.detail: Major risk factor for SCC; moderate risk factor for adenocarcinoma. Risk for SCC decreases substantially after cessation, but adenocarcinoma risk remains elevated for years [11-15].
Strongly associated with SCC, acts synergistically with tobacco. Not a major risk factor for adenocarcinoma [11,13].
Established as the strongest risk factor for adenocarcinoma, contributes to GERD and Barrett esophagus [12,16,17].
Major underlying cause of esophageal adenocarcinoma through development of Barrett esophagus [18-20].
Metaplastic columnar epithelium; confers a 30- to 60-fold increased risk of adenocarcinoma [19,21].
Risk increases markedly after age 50 for both histologies, particularly for adenocarcinoma [22-24,75].
Males have higher incidence of both SCC and adenocarcinoma; male sex is an independent risk factor for BE and adenocarcinoma [22-24,75].
Adenocarcinoma is more common in non-Hispanic whites; SCC is more common in African Americans and certain Asian populations [7,8].
Tylosis (RHBDF2 mutations) → SCC; Fanconi anemia, Bloom syndrome → SCC; Familial Barrett esophagus → adenocarcinoma [26-28,31-33,40,43,44].

risk_factors.factor: Personal history of head and neck cancer
risk_factors.detail: Increases risk for SCC of the esophagus, likely due to shared exposure to tobacco/alcohol [ESOPH-A 1 of 8].
Chronic irritation of the esophageal mucosa increases risk for SCC [ESOPH-A 1 of 8].

protective_factors: Smoking cessation: Risk for esophageal SCC decreases substantially after quitting smoking; however, the risk for adenocarcinoma remains unchanged even years after cessation [14,15].

Weight control and healthy diet: Although not explicitly listed as a protective factor in the source, maintaining a healthy body weight is recommended in survivorship to reduce cancer risk [ESOPH-J 3 of 4].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: The classic presentation of esophageal or esophagogastric junction (EGJ) cancer is dominated by progressive dysphagia, often first noticed with solid foods and later progressing to liquids. Dysphagia is the most common symptom, occurring in most patients at diagnosis [ESOPH-H 1 of 3]. Patients frequently report unintentional weight loss, which may be substantial, as well as odynophagia (painful swallowing). Symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) are common in patients with adenocarcinoma, particularly those with underlying Barrett esophagus [MS-7]. Other presenting features include nausea, vomiting, and regurgitation. Up to 50% of patients have locoregional disease at diagnosis, while nearly 50% have cancer that extends beyond locoregional confines [MS-7]. The global incidence varies 60-fold between high- and low-incidence regions, with squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) more common in Eastern Europe and Asia, and adenocarcinoma more common in North America and Western Europe [MS-2].

symptoms.name: Dysphagia
symptoms.detail: Progressive difficulty swallowing, initially with solid foods then liquids. Graded on a 0–4 scale: 0 = able to eat solid food normally; 1 = able to swallow food cut into small pieces; 2 = able to swallow semisolid food; 3 = able to swallow liquids only; 4 = unable to swallow liquids or saliva [ESOPH-H 1 of 3].
symptoms.frequency: Most common symptom, present in the majority of patients at diagnosis [MS-2].
symptoms.alarm: Yes – progressive dysphagia is an alarm symptom requiring urgent endoscopic evaluation.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss, often significant, due to decreased oral intake and catabolic effects of malignancy.
symptoms.frequency: Very common, especially in advanced disease. Weight loss >10% in 3 months is a poor prognostic sign.
symptoms.alarm: Yes – weight loss >10% in 3 months or severe malnutrition requires urgent nutritional assessment.

symptoms.name: Odynophagia
symptoms.detail: Pain on swallowing, often described as a burning or sharp sensation.
symptoms.alarm: Yes – may indicate deep tumor invasion or esophageal perforation.

symptoms.name: GERD symptoms
symptoms.detail: Heartburn, regurgitation, and acid reflux are frequent, especially in patients with Barrett esophagus.
symptoms.frequency: Very common in adenocarcinoma; present in most patients with Barrett esophagus.
symptoms.alarm: Yes – new or worsening GERD symptoms, especially in patients with risk factors, should prompt endoscopy.

symptoms.name: Nausea and vomiting
symptoms.detail: May be due to luminal obstruction or tumor-related dysmotility.
symptoms.frequency: Common in advanced disease with significant obstruction.
symptoms.alarm: Yes – may indicate complete esophageal obstruction requiring urgent decompression.

symptoms.name: Hematemesis or melena
symptoms.detail: Bleeding from tumor surface, which can be chronic (iron deficiency anemia) or acute (pre-terminal aorto-esophageal fistula).
symptoms.frequency: Less common but serious; chronic blood loss occurs in some patients.
symptoms.alarm: Yes – acute bleeding may be pre-terminal; endoscopic assessment is cautious [ESOPH-H 3 of 3].

signs.name: Weight loss and cachexia
signs.detail: Physical wasting, loss of muscle mass, and subcutaneous fat.
signs.frequency: Common at presentation; may be severe.

signs.name: Pallor and signs of anemia
signs.detail: Pale conjunctivae, fatigue, and weakness due to chronic blood loss or poor nutrition.
signs.frequency: Common in patients with chronic bleeding.

signs.name: Palpable lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Supraclavicular (Virchow node) or cervical lymph nodes may be palpable in advanced disease.
signs.frequency: Less common at initial presentation; more frequent in metastatic disease.

signs.name: Abdominal mass or hepatomegaly
signs.detail: Palpable mass in epigastrium or right upper quadrant if liver metastases are present.

atypical_or_missed: Early-stage esophageal cancer (pTis, pT1a) is often asymptomatic and detected incidentally during screening endoscopy for Barrett esophagus or GERD [ESOPH-A 1 of 8].
Patients with Barrett esophagus may present only with longstanding GERD symptoms; dysplasia may be found on surveillance biopsies without any new symptoms [MS-7].

Some patients present with complications such as aspiration pneumonia (due to tracheoesophageal fistula) or hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement) as the first manifestation [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
Weight loss may be attributed to other causes (e.g., diet, depression) delaying diagnosis until dysphagia becomes prominent.

red_flags: Progressive dysphagia from solids to liquids (Grade 3 or 4 dysphagia) [ESOPH-H 1 of 3].
Unintentional weight loss >10% of body weight within 3 months.
Odynophagia, especially if severe or persistent.
Acute gastrointestinal bleeding (hematemesis or melena) – may be pre-terminal if associated with aorto-esophageal fistula [ESOPH-H 3 of 3].
New hoarseness or cough – may indicate recurrent laryngeal nerve or tracheal invasion (cT4b disease) [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
Severe uncontrolled pain after esophageal stent placement – requires immediate endoscopic removal [ESOPH-H 2 of 3].
Complete esophageal obstruction (Grade 4 dysphagia) – requires urgent endoscopic lumen restoration or enteral access [ESOPH-H 2 of 3].
New onset of cough after swallowing (suggests tracheoesophageal fistula) – emergency evaluation needed [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].

diagnostic.rationale: Gold standard for diagnosis. Allows direct visualization of tumor, documentation of location, size, circumferential involvement, and degree of obstruction. At least 6–8 biopsies using standard forceps are needed for histologic and molecular interpretation [ESOPH-A 1 of 8].
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic confirmation of SCC or adenocarcinoma; presence of Barrett esophagus; depth of invasion in early lesions (pTis, pT1a, pT1b).

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for locoregional staging. Determines depth of tumor invasion (T stage) and identifies regional lymph nodes (N stage). Accuracy is improved with fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of suspicious nodes [ESOPH-A 4 of 8].
diagnostic.key_findings: Hypoechoic expansion of esophageal wall layers correlating with T category; hypoechoic, rounded, well-circumscribed lymph nodes suggestive of malignancy.

diagnostic.rationale: Provides precise histologic assessment of depth of invasion for Tis, T1a, and selected T1b tumors (≤2 cm). May be therapeutic if complete excision is achieved [ESOPH-A 5 of 8].
diagnostic.key_findings: Depth of submucosal invasion in μm; lymphovascular invasion (LVI); differentiation grade; margin status.

staging.test: Chest/abdomen CT with oral and IV contrast
staging.rationale: Assesses location and extent of primary tumor, proximity to mediastinal structures, and detection of distant metastases (M stage). Pelvic CT is added if clinically indicated [ESOPH-1].
staging.indication: All patients for initial staging; also for response assessment ≥5–8 weeks after chemoradiation [ESOPH-5A].

staging.rationale: Superior to CT alone for detecting distant metastases (M1). Improves detection of occult metastatic disease and helps select patients for curative resection. Limited role in T staging [MS-5].
staging.indication: Recommended if no evidence of M1 disease on CT; not indicated for early-stage (cT1) tumors due to high false-positive rate [MS-6].

staging.rationale: Required if tumor is at or above the carina to assess for tracheal or bronchial invasion (cT4b). Includes biopsy of any abnormalities and cytology of washings [ESOPH-1].
staging.indication: Tumor located at or above carina with no evidence of M1 disease.

staging.rationale: Detects radiographically occult peritoneal metastases (M1). Positive cytology defines M1 disease and indicates poor prognosis [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
staging.indication: Consider for Siewert II/III tumors, advanced T3 or N+ disease.

staging.rationale: Confirms diagnosis and allows biomarker testing.
staging.indication: As clinically indicated to document metastatic disease.

biomarkers.rationale: Universal testing recommended for all newly diagnosed patients. MSI-H/dMMR status guides immunotherapy (pembrolizumab, dostarlimab, nivolumab) [ESOPH-B 5 of 7].

Universal testing recommended for all newly diagnosed patients to determine eligibility for PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, tislelizumab). CPS and TAP scores are interchangeable [ESOPH-B 5 of 7].
Recommended for advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma to select trastuzumab-based therapy. IHC first, followed by ISH if 2+ [ESOPH-B 3 of 7].
biomarkers.when: If advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented or suspected.

biomarkers.rationale: For advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma to select zolbetuximab therapy. Positivity if ≥75% viable tumor cells have moderate to strong (2+/3+) membranous staining [ESOPH-B 4 of 7].
biomarkers.when: If advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented or suspected.

biomarkers.rationale: Detects ERBB2 amplification, MSI, TMB, NTRK fusions, RET fusions, BRAF V600E. Consider for comprehensive profiling when tissue is limited [ESOPH-B 6 of 7].
biomarkers.when: Consider for all newly diagnosed patients; may be used later in clinical course.

biomarkers.rationale: May identify targetable alterations when tissue is insufficient or for monitoring disease progression. Negative result does not exclude tumor [ESOPH-B 6 of 7].
biomarkers.when: When limited tissue or patient unable to undergo traditional biopsy.

do_not_miss: EGD with multiple biopsies (6–8) – essential for histologic diagnosis and biomarker testing [ESOPH-A 1 of 8].
EUS with FNA of suspicious lymph nodes – critical for accurate N staging [ESOPH-A 4 of 8].
ER for early-stage lesions (≤2 cm) – provides precise depth invasion and may be therapeutic [ESOPH-A 5 of 8].
FDG-PET/CT for M staging in patients without definite M1 disease – prevents unnecessary surgery [MS-5].
Bronchoscopy for tumors at or above carina – to exclude tracheal invasion [ESOPH-1].
Universal MSI/MMR and PD-L1 testing in all newly diagnosed patients [ESOPH-B 5 of 7].
avoid: Routine use of FDG-PET/CT for T staging – limited accuracy for T1/T2 tumors and high false-positive rate [MS-6].
EUS alone for distinguishing T1a from T1b – ER is more accurate [MS-6].
Blind biopsies without adequate sampling (fewer than 6 biopsies) – insufficient for histologic and molecular interpretation [ESOPH-A 1 of 8].
Percutaneous gastrostomy tube placement in surgical candidates without multidisciplinary discussion – may compromise gastric conduit for reconstruction [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
Routine use of FDG-PET to select patients for surgery after chemoradiation – cannot distinguish microscopic residual disease [MS-11].

staging.system: AJCC 8th edition, TNM system for carcinoma of the esophagus and esophagogastric junction (effective 2017) [ST-1].

t_stages.description: High-grade dysplasia (HGD) / carcinoma in situ – neoplastic cells confined to epithelium by basement membrane
Tumor invades lamina propria, muscularis mucosae, or submucosa
Tumor invades lamina propria or muscularis mucosae
Tumor invades pleura, pericardium, azygos vein, diaphragm, or peritoneum
Tumor invades other adjacent structures (e.g., aorta, vertebral body, airway)

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locoregional disease invading lamina propria/submucosa with up to 2 positive nodes

stage_groups.criteria: cT2 N0-1 M0 or cT3 N0 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locoregional disease involving muscularis propria or adventitia, node-negative or up to 2 positive nodes

stage_groups.criteria: cT3 N1 M0 or cT1-3 N2 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locoregional disease with adventitial invasion and 1-6 positive nodes

stage_groups.criteria: cT4 N0-2 M0 or Any T N3 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced unresectable (cT4b) or bulky nodal disease (N3)

stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative systemic therapy and best supportive care

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locoregional disease invading lamina propria/submucosa, node-negative

stage_groups.criteria: cT2 N1 M0, cT3 N0-1 M0, cT4a N0-1 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locoregional disease with variable T and N involvement, up to T4a

stage_groups.criteria: cT1-4a N2 M0, cT4b N0-2 M0, Any T N3 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced unresectable (cT4b) or bulky nodal disease

stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative systemic therapy and best supportive care

staging_pearls: Clinical staging (cTNM) is used for newly diagnosed, untreated patients; pathologic staging (pTNM) for resection without prior therapy; postneoadjuvant staging (ypTNM) for those receiving preoperative therapy [MS-5].
For SCC, location (upper/middle/lower) and histologic grade (G1–G3) are incorporated into pathologic stage groups but not into clinical stage groups [ST-2].
For adenocarcinoma, only histologic grade is used in pathologic staging; location does not affect stage [ST-3].
EGJ tumors with epicenter ≤2 cm into proximal stomach are staged as esophageal adenocarcinomas; those >2 cm into stomach are staged as gastric carcinomas [MS-5].
Celiac nodal involvement in EGJ/distal esophageal cancers may still be considered for combined modality therapy [ESOPH-1A].
At least 16 regional lymph nodes should be removed and pathologically examined for curative-intent esophagectomy; >30 nodes is desirable [ESOPH-B 2 of 7, ESOPH-C 2 of 3].
Survival decreases with increasing pT, pN, pM, histologic grade, and age. Survival is better for adenocarcinoma than SCC in the same stage [MS-5].

management_principles.overview: The management of esophageal and esophagogastric junction (EGJ) cancers requires a multidisciplinary approach integrating surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, gastroenterology, radiology, and pathology. Combined modality therapy is supported by category 1 evidence demonstrating efficacy for localized esophagogastric cancer [ESOPH-E]. Treatment philosophy emphasizes individualized care based on histology (squamous cell carcinoma [SCC] vs. adenocarcinoma), stage, performance status, and molecular biomarkers (PD-L1, MSI/MMR, HER2, CLDN18.2). The goal of therapy is curative for locoregional disease and palliative for advanced/metastatic disease. Perioperative systemic therapy (category 1) is preferred for patients with resectable esophageal or EGJ adenocarcinoma who are medically fit, while preoperative chemoradiation is preferred for SCC [ESOPH-F 1 of 12]. Definitive chemoradiation is reserved for unresectable disease or non-surgical candidates. Best supportive care is integral at all stages, especially for palliation [ESOPH-H].

by_intent.patient_group: Medically fit, resectable locoregional disease (Tis–T4a, N0–N+, M0)
by_intent.approach: Multimodality therapy: endoscopic therapy (ER ± ablation) for early-stage (pTis, pT1a, selected pT1b); esophagectomy for T1b or deeper; preoperative chemoradiation (SCC, category 1; adenocarcinoma, category 1 preferred); perioperative chemotherapy (adenocarcinoma, category 1 preferred); definitive chemoradiation for non-surgical candidates or unresectable T4b. Adjuvant nivolumab for residual disease after preoperative chemoradiation (category 1) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12].

by_intent.patient_group: Unresectable locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic disease
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (first-line and subsequent) based on PS, histology, and biomarkers; palliative radiotherapy; endoscopic stenting for dysphagia; best supportive care. For PS 0–2 (Karnofsky ≥60% or ECOG ≤2), systemic therapy with or without palliative/best supportive care. For PS ≥3 (Karnofsky <60% or ECOG ≥3), best supportive care alone [ESOPH-10, ESOPH-21].

by_intent.patient_group: Non-surgical candidates (medically unfit or decline surgery) with locoregional disease
by_intent.approach: Definitive chemoradiation (preferred) for T1b–T4a, any N; endoscopic therapy (ER ± ablation) for pTis, pT1a, superficial pT1b; palliative RT or best supportive care for those unable to tolerate chemoradiation. For tumors with poor prognostic features (LVI, poor differentiation, positive margins, tumor diameter ≥2 cm), consider definitive chemoradiation [ESOPH-8, ESOPH-19].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: The NCCN Panel recommends an infrastructure that encourages multidisciplinary treatment decision-making by all disciplines involved in the care of patients with localized esophagogastric cancer. Frequent meetings (weekly or biweekly) with participation of surgical oncology, medical oncology, gastroenterology, radiation oncology, radiology, and pathology are encouraged. Additional support from nutritional services, social workers, nurses, and palliative care specialists is desirable. Joint review of actual medical data is more effective than reading reports. A brief documentation of consensus recommendations is useful. Re-presentation of select patient outcomes and periodic formal review of relevant literature are encouraged [ESOPH-E].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is a key determinant of treatment intensity. The guideline uses Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS) and ECOG PS. For unresectable locally advanced or metastatic disease: KPS ≥60% or ECOG PS ≤2 → systemic therapy and/or palliative/best supportive care. KPS <60% or ECOG PS ≥3 → palliative/best supportive care alone [ESOPH-10, ESOPH-21]. Borderline PS patients may be considered for modified or reduced-intensity regimens. In the perioperative setting, patients must be medically fit to tolerate major surgery [ESOPH-2, ESOPH-11]. The FLOT regimen is reserved for those with good PS due to toxicity [ESOPH-F 1 of 12].

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma: pTis, pT1a, superficial pT1b – Endoscopic Therapy

options.label: Endoscopic resection (ER) ± ablation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A; ER preferred for complete excision; ablation may be used for multifocal HGD/carcinoma in situ [ESOPH-3]

options.label: Ablation alone
options.recommendation: For selected patients; low evidence for ablation of SCC after ER [ESOPH-3]

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: For extensive Tis or if not controlled by endoscopic therapy [ESOPH-3]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy needed if complete excision and no high-risk features.

nodes.criteria: pT1a (invasion into lamina propria or muscularis mucosae), N0

options.label: ER ± ablation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A; ER followed by ablation to eliminate residual dysplasia [ESOPH-3]

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: For patients with extensive or nodular disease not controlled by ER/ablation [ESOPH-3]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy if ER is curative (no LVI, well/moderate differentiation, negative margins).

nodes.criteria: Superficial pT1b (≤200 μm invasion), N0, low-risk features

options.label: ER ± ablation
options.recommendation: Category 2A; for selected superficial pT1b without LVI, well/moderate differentiation, size ≤2 cm [ESOPH-3, ESOPH-A 5 of 8]

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Standard for pT1b; discussion of risk of esophagectomy vs. nodal disease required [ESOPH-3]

adjuvant_logic.summary: If ER with curative features, observation; otherwise consider esophagectomy or additional therapy.

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Locoregional Disease – Medically Fit (cT1b–T4a, N0–N+)

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2A; transhiatal or transthoracic, minimally invasive, gastric reconstruction [ESOPH-4]

adjuvant_logic.summary: If R0 resection, observation. If R1/R2, fluoropyrimidine-based chemoradiation [ESOPH-6].

nodes.criteria: cT2, N0 (high-risk: LVI, ≥3 cm, poorly differentiated) OR cT1b–cT2, N+ OR cT3–cT4a, any N

options.label: Preoperative chemoradiation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 1 for SCC; preferred regimens: paclitaxel/carboplatin (CROSS), fluorouracil/oxaliplatin (FOLFOX), fluorouracil/cisplatin [ESOPH-4, ESOPH-F 3 of 12]

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: For patients who decline surgery; dose 50–50.4 Gy [ESOPH-4]

options.label: Chemotherapy alone
options.recommendation: Consider in setting of invasion of trachea, great vessels, or heart (T4b) [ESOPH-4]

adjuvant_logic.summary: After preoperative chemoradiation: response assessment with PET/CT ± EGD; if no evidence of disease, esophagectomy or observation (category 2B); if persistent local disease, esophagectomy preferred. After surgery: if R0 and yp T0 N0, observation; if yp T+ and/or N+, nivolumab (category 1) [ESOPH-5, ESOPH-7].

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma: cT4b (Unresectable) – Medically Fit
branching_basis: T4b (invasion of heart, great vessels, trachea)

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Category 2A; may occasionally facilitate surgical resection in select patients [ESOPH-4]

options.label: Chemotherapy alone
options.recommendation: Consider in setting of invasion of trachea, great vessels, or heart [ESOPH-4]

options.label: Endoluminal stenting
options.recommendation: For select patients as appropriate [ESOPH-4]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative management if unresectable after therapy; if response allows resection, proceed to esophagectomy [ESOPH-5].

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Management for Nonsurgical Candidates

options.label: ER ± ablation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A; ER may be therapeutic; ablation alone may be used [ESOPH-8]

options.label: Ablation alone
options.recommendation: For selected pTis [ESOPH-8]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Endoscopic surveillance after eradication.

options.label: ER ± ablation
options.recommendation: Category 2A; for superficial pT1b without high-risk features [ESOPH-8]

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Consider for tumors with poor prognostic features (LVI, poorly differentiated, submucosal invasion ≥200 μm, positive margins, diameter ≥2 cm) [ESOPH-8]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Endoscopic surveillance or follow-up after chemoradiation.

nodes.criteria: cT1b–T4a, N0–N+ OR cT4b (unresectable), able to tolerate chemoradiation

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Category 2A; dose 50–50.4 Gy [ESOPH-8]

options.label: Palliative RT
options.recommendation: For those unable to tolerate full chemoradiation [ESOPH-8]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up/surveillance as per ESOPH-9; if persistent local disease, consider salvage esophagectomy if medically fit.

options.recommendation: Category 2A; includes stenting, RT, nutritional support [ESOPH-8]

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma: pTis, pT1a, superficial pT1b – Endoscopic Therapy

options.label: ER ± ablation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A; ER preferred for complete removal; ablation for residual BE [ESOPH-12]

options.label: Ablation alone
options.recommendation: For selected cases [ESOPH-12]

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: For extensive Tis or nodular disease not controlled by endoscopic therapy [ESOPH-12]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy if complete eradication.

options.label: ER ± ablation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A; ER followed by ablation to eliminate residual BE [ESOPH-12]

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: For patients with extensive or nodular disease [ESOPH-12]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy if ER curative.

nodes.criteria: Superficial pT1b (≤500 μm invasion, low-risk), N0

options.label: ER ± ablation
options.recommendation: Category 2A; for low-risk tumors: ≤2 cm, well/moderate differentiation, no LVI, negative margins [ESOPH-12, ESOPH-A 5 of 8]

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Standard for pT1b; discussion of risk/benefit [ESOPH-12]

adjuvant_logic.summary: If ER with curative features, observation; otherwise esophagectomy.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma: Locoregional Disease – Medically Fit (cT1b–T4a, N0–N+)

options.label: Perioperative systemic therapy (category 1, preferred)
options.recommendation: FLOT (FLOT4 trial) or FOLFOX/CAPOX; preferred for adenocarcinoma [ESOPH-13]

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2A; transhiatal or transthoracic, minimally invasive [ESOPH-13]

options.label: Preoperative chemoradiation
options.recommendation: For borderline resectable or patient not candidate for FLOT; may be considered [ESOPH-13, ESOPH-13A]

adjuvant_logic.summary: If esophagectomy alone: pT3,T4a or N+ → consider chemotherapy or chemoradiation (fluoropyrimidine-based) [ESOPH-17]. If perioperative therapy: continue postoperative systemic therapy per protocol.

nodes.criteria: cT2, N0 (high-risk: LVI, ≥3 cm, poorly differentiated) OR cT1b–cT2, N+ OR cT3–cT4a, any N

options.label: Perioperative systemic therapy (category 1, preferred)
options.recommendation: FLOT for selected patients with good PS; FOLFOX/CAPOX for others [ESOPH-13]

options.label: Preoperative chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Category 1; preferred regimens: paclitaxel/carboplatin, FOLFOX, fluorouracil/cisplatin [ESOPH-13]

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Only for patients who decline surgery [ESOPH-13]

options.label: Neoadjuvant or perioperative immune checkpoint inhibitor
options.recommendation: Consider if tumor is MSI-H/dMMR; options: dostarlimab (neoadjuvant only), nivolumab+ipilimumab, pembrolizumab, tremelimumab+durvalumab [ESOPH-13, ESOPH-F 3 of 12]

adjuvant_logic.summary: After perioperative therapy: response assessment; if no evidence of disease and patient declines surgery or unresectable, definitive chemoradiation; if surgery, postoperative management based on pathology: yp T0 N0 → systemic therapy if perioperatively received (category 1) or observation; yp T+/N+ → nivolumab (if preoperative chemoradiation) or systemic therapy if perioperatively received [ESOPH-14, ESOPH-18]. After preoperative chemoradiation: response assessment; if no evidence of disease, esophagectomy or observation (category 2B); if persistent local disease, esophagectomy preferred [ESOPH-15].

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma: cT4b (Unresectable) – Medically Fit
branching_basis: T4b (invasion of heart, great vessels, trachea)

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Category 2A; may facilitate surgery in select patients [ESOPH-13]

options.label: Chemotherapy alone
options.recommendation: Consider in setting of invasion of trachea, great vessels, or heart [ESOPH-13]

options.label: Systemic therapy and/or palliative care
options.recommendation: For non-surgical candidates [ESOPH-21]

adjuvant_logic.summary: If response to definitive chemoradiation allows resection, consider esophagectomy [ESOPH-15]. Otherwise palliative management.

options.label: ER ± ablation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A; ER followed by ablation to eliminate residual BE [ESOPH-19]

options.label: Ablation alone
options.recommendation: For pTis [ESOPH-19]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Endoscopic surveillance.

options.label: ER ± ablation
options.recommendation: For superficial pT1b without high-risk features [ESOPH-19]

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Consider for tumors with poor prognostic features (LVI, poorly differentiated, submucosal invasion >500 μm, positive margins, diameter ≥2 cm) [ESOPH-19]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Endoscopic surveillance or follow-up after chemoradiation.

nodes.criteria: cT1b–T4a, N0–N+ OR cT4b (unresectable), able to tolerate chemoradiation

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Category 2A; dose 50–50.4 Gy [ESOPH-19]

options.label: Palliative RT
options.recommendation: For those unable to tolerate full chemoradiation [ESOPH-19]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up/surveillance as per ESOPH-20; if persistent local disease, consider salvage esophagectomy if medically fit.

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Category 2A; includes stenting, RT, nutritional support [ESOPH-19]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No further disease-directed therapy.

nodes.criteria: MSI-H/dMMR, cT2–T4a, any N, medically fit

options.label: Neoadjuvant or perioperative immune checkpoint inhibitor(s)
options.recommendation: Consider in consultation with multidisciplinary team. Options: dostarlimab (neoadjuvant only, category 2A), nivolumab+ipilimumab, pembrolizumab, tremelimumab+durvalumab [ESOPH-13, ESOPH-13A, ESOPH-F 3 of 12]. Note: esophagectomy remains standard approach outside prospective trials; non-operative management requires immunotherapy for ≥1 year and intensive surveillance.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Response assessment ≥5-8 weeks after neoadjuvant ICI: PET/CT, chest/abdomen CT, EGD+biopsy. If no evidence of disease, observation or esophagectomy; if persistent local disease, esophagectomy preferred [ESOPH-16]. If non-operative management, continue immunotherapy (total ≥1 year) and surveillance every 12 weeks for 2 years then every 6 months to year 5 [ESOPH-13A].

management_pathways.title: Recurrent or Metastatic Disease: Palliative Management

nodes.criteria: Unresectable locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic; PS 0-2

options.label: Systemic therapy (first-line based on biomarkers)
options.recommendation: Adenocarcinoma HER2+ : fluoropyrimidine+oxaliplatin+trastuzumab ± pembrolizumab (if CPS ≥1). Adenocarcinoma HER2- : fluoro+oxaliplatin + nivolumab (CPS ≥5), pembrolizumab (CPS ≥10), tislelizumab (CPS ≥5), or zolbetuximab (CLDN18.2+). SCC: fluoro+oxaliplatin+ nivolumab/pembrolizumab/tislelizumab (CPS ≥1), or nivolumab+ipilimumab. MSI-H: pembrolizumab or dostarlimab or nivolumab+ipilimumab [ESOPH-F 4 of 12, ESOPH-F 5 of 12].

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Integrate with systemic therapy; includes management of dysphagia, obstruction, pain, bleeding [ESOPH-H]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line or subsequent therapy based on prior therapy and PS: e.g., ramucirumab+paclitaxel, fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan (HER2+), nivolumab (SCC), pembrolizumab (SCC CPS≥10), docetaxel, paclitaxel, irinotecan, trifluridine/tipiracil (EGJ 3rd-line). Useful in certain circumstances: entrectinib/larotrectinib (NTRK), dabrafenib/trametinib (BRAF V600E), selpercatinib (RET) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12, ESOPH-F 7 of 12].

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Category 2A; no systemic therapy [ESOPH-10, ESOPH-21]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Symptom management only.

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence after esophagectomy (no prior chemoradiation)

options.label: Concurrent chemoradiation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A; fluoropyrimidine-based [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20]

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: If resectable and medically fit [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20]

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Category 2A [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20]

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Category 2A [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20]

adjuvant_logic.summary: After treatment, response assessment with CT.

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence after prior chemoradiation (no esophagectomy)

options.label: Esophagectomy if resectable and medically operable
options.recommendation: Category 2A; salvage esophagectomy [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20]

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: If unresectable or medically inoperable [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20]

adjuvant_logic.summary: If esophagectomy, manage as per postoperative pathway.

options.label: Systemic therapy or Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Per PS and biomarker status [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-10, ESOPH-20, ESOPH-21]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Further treatment after two sequential regimens depends on PS and clinical trials availability [ESOPH-10, ESOPH-21].

items.text: History and physical (H&P)
items.recommendation: Essential for all patients at initial presentation [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Assess for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, including social determinants of health [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-1A]
items.recommendation: Category 2A; recommended for all newly diagnosed patients [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Nutritional assessment and counseling
items.recommendation: Recommended for all patients to optimize nutritional status before and during therapy [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Smoking cessation advice, counseling, and pharmacotherapy as indicated
items.recommendation: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-1A]

items.text: Screen for family history to identify hereditary cancer predisposition syndromes
items.recommendation: Refer to Principles of Genetic Risk Assessment (ESOPH-D); consider referral to genetics counselor [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with biopsy
items.recommendation: Multiple biopsies (6-8) using standard forceps; larger forceps for BE surveillance; wide-area transepithelial sampling may be adjunct for BE [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-A 1 of 8]

items.text: Chest/abdomen CT with oral and IV contrast
items.recommendation: Initial staging; pelvis CT with contrast as clinically indicated [ESOPH-1]

items.text: FDG-PET/CT evaluation (skull base to mid-thigh) if no evidence of M1 disease
items.recommendation: For detection of occult metastases; not indicated if M1 already confirmed [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) if no evidence of M1 unresectable disease
items.recommendation: Important for T and N staging; FNA of suspicious nodes if can be done safely [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Endoscopic resection (ER) for accurate staging of early-stage cancers (Tis, T1a, T1b)
items.recommendation: Recommended for small nodular lesions ≤2 cm; provides accurate depth of invasion [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-A]

items.text: Bronchoscopy if tumor at or above carina with no evidence of M1 disease
items.recommendation: To assess airway involvement [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Universal testing for MSI by PCR/NGS or MMR by IHC in all newly diagnosed patients
items.recommendation: Category 2A; for all newly diagnosed esophageal and EGJ cancers [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-B 5 of 7]

items.text: Universal testing for PD-L1 by IHC in all newly diagnosed patients
items.recommendation: Category 2A; using companion diagnostic; CPS ≥1 considered positive [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-B 5 of 7]

items.text: HER2 (ERBB2) testing if advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented/suspected
items.recommendation: IHC first, then ISH if equivocal; IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+ considered positive [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-B 3 of 7]

items.text: CLDN18.2 testing if advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented/suspected
items.recommendation: IHC; ≥75% viable tumor cells with moderate to strong staining (2+/3+) considered positive [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-B 4 of 7]

items.text: NGS should be considered via validated assay
items.recommendation: For detection of targetable alterations (ERBB2, BRAF, NTRK, RET, TMB, MSI) when limited tissue or for disease progression monitoring [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Histologic classification (SCC vs. adenocarcinoma)
items.recommendation: Essential for staging and treatment decisions; mixed adenosquamous staged as SCC [ESOPH-B 1 of 7]

items.text: Assign Siewert category for EGJ adenocarcinomas
items.recommendation: Siewert I (epicenter 1-5 cm above EGJ), II (1 cm above to 2 cm below), III (2-5 cm below); Types I/II managed as esophageal, Type III as gastric [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-C 1 of 3]

items.text: Assessment of treatment response in resection specimens after preoperative therapy
items.recommendation: Use modified Ryan scheme (tumor regression score 0-3); include lymph nodes in regression score [ESOPH-B 2 of 7]

items.text: Complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive chemistry profile
items.recommendation: Baseline assessment [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Biopsy of metastatic disease as clinically indicated
items.recommendation: To confirm diagnosis and for biomarker testing [ESOPH-1]

items.text: Consider enteric feeding tube for preoperative nutritional support in surgical candidates
items.recommendation: Percutaneous gastrostomy may be considered for cervical esophageal tumors receiving definitive chemoradiation or marginally resectable disease [ESOPH-2, ESOPH-11]

items.text: Laparoscopy (optional) for EGJ adenocarcinoma if no M1 disease
items.recommendation: Useful to detect occult peritoneal metastases, especially for Siewert II/III [ESOPH-11]

surgery.role: Surgery is a major component of treatment for locoregional esophageal and EGJ cancers, with curative intent for resectable disease. Esophagectomy should be considered for all patients who are physiologically fit with resectable esophageal cancer (>5 cm from cricopharyngeus). Cervical or cervicothoracic cancers <5 cm from cricopharyngeus should be treated with definitive chemoradiation [ESOPH-C 1 of 3]. Surgery also has a role in salvage after definitive chemoradiation for localized recurrence and in carefully selected patients for palliation [ESOPH-C 2 of 3].
principles: Prior to surgery, clinical staging should include CT chest/abdomen, whole-body FDG-PET (integrated FDG-PET/CT preferred), and EUS [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
All patients should be assessed by an esophageal surgeon for physiologic ability to undergo esophageal resection [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
Siewert tumor type should be assessed in all EGJ adenocarcinomas. Type I and II are treated per esophageal guidelines; Type III per gastric cancer guidelines [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
Laparoscopy may be useful for detecting occult metastatic disease, especially for Siewert II/III tumors. Positive peritoneal cytology is M1 disease [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].
Strong consideration should be given to minimally invasive esophagectomy approaches due to improved postoperative outcomes compared to open surgery [ESOPH-C 2 of 3].

In patients unable to swallow during induction therapy, esophageal dilatation or feeding jejunostomy tube (J-tube) is preferred over gastrostomy, which may compromise gastric conduit [ESOPH-C 2 of 3].
Enteral nutritional support via jejunostomy feeding tube is preferred for preoperative nutritional support [ESOPH-C 2 of 3].

A thorough lymph node dissection should be performed. For patients without induction chemoradiation, at least 16 lymph nodes should be removed and assessed. Optimum number after preoperative chemoradiation is unknown, but similar lymph node resection is recommended [ESOPH-C 2 of 3].
Patients with potentially resectable esophageal cancer should undergo multidisciplinary review. Surgery should be performed in high-volume centers by experienced surgeons [ESOPH-C 2 of 3].

procedures.name: Ivor Lewis esophagogastrectomy
procedures.approach: Laparotomy + right thoracotomy; anastomosis in upper chest at or above azygos vein
procedures.indications: Distal esophageal and EGJ tumors; most commonly used transthoracic approach [ESOPH-C 2 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, pulmonary complications, gastric emptying delay

procedures.name: McKeown esophagogastrectomy
procedures.indications: Tumors in upper, middle, and lower thoracic esophagus; allows more proximal margin [ESOPH-C 2 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, anastomotic leak (cervical less severe), stricture

procedures.name: Transhiatal esophagogastrectomy
procedures.approach: Laparotomy + cervical anastomosis; blunt dissection of thoracic esophagus
procedures.indications: Distal esophageal and EGJ tumors; not recommended as routine approach due to lower lymph node retrieval [ESOPH-C 2 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Higher anastomotic leak (cervical), recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, less mediastinal dissection

procedures.name: Minimally invasive esophagectomy (MIE)
procedures.approach: Laparoscopic/laparotomy + thoracoscopic/thoracotomy; options: Ivor Lewis, McKeown, robotic-assisted
procedures.indications: Resectable esophageal or EGJ cancer; preferred over open when feasible [ESOPH-C 2 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Lower pulmonary infection rate; similar anastomotic leak risk; requires experienced surgeon

procedures.name: Left transthoracic or thoracoabdominal esophagectomy
procedures.approach: Left thoracotomy through 8th intercostal space ± abdominal incision; anastomosis in left chest
procedures.indications: Distal esophageal and bulky EGJ tumors [ESOPH-C 2 of 3]
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, pulmonary complications, cardiac injury

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectable: T1–T3 tumors (even with regional nodal metastases, though bulky multi-station N3 is relative contraindication), T4a tumors (involvement of pericardium, pleura, diaphragm). Unresectable: cT4b tumors (involvement of heart, great vessels, trachea, adjacent organs like liver, pancreas, lung, spleen); most patients with multi-station bulky lymphadenopathy; EGJ tumors with supraclavicular lymph node involvement; distant metastases (including non-regional lymph nodes) [ESOPH-C 1 of 3]. Cervical esophageal tumors <5 cm from cricopharyngeus are treated with definitive chemoradiation [ESOPH-C 1 of 3].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is integral to the management of esophageal and EGJ cancers, used in preoperative (neoadjuvant), definitive, postoperative (adjuvant), and palliative settings. Treatment recommendations should be made after multidisciplinary consultation using all available diagnostic information (CT, barium swallow, EUS, endoscopy, FDG-PET/CT) [ESOPH-G 1 of 5]. Siewert I/II tumors managed with RT for esophageal cancer; Siewert III managed per gastric cancer guidelines [ESOPH-G 1 of 5].
principles: CT simulation and conformal treatment planning should be used with 3D-CRT or IMRT. Proton beam therapy is appropriate when dose reduction to organs at risk (heart, lungs) is needed, ideally within a clinical trial or registry [ESOPH-G 1 of 5].
Treat patients in supine position for reproducibility [ESOPH-G 1 of 5].
Use immobilization device. For distal esophageal/EGJ lesions, consider 4D-CT planning for respiratory motion management [ESOPH-G 1 of 5].

Target volume: GTV includes primary tumor and involved regional lymph nodes; CTV includes 3-4 cm superior/inferior expansion along esophagus and 1 cm radial, plus nodal CTV with 0.5-1.5 cm expansion; PTV expansion 0.5-1 cm accounting for respiratory motion [ESOPH-G 2 of 5].

Elective nodal coverage depends on tumor location: cervical esophagus – supraclavicular and higher echelon cervical nodes if N≥1; proximal third – para-esophageal and supraclavicular; middle third – para-esophageal; distal third/EGJ – para-esophageal, lesser curvature, splenic nodes, celiac axis [ESOPH-G 2 of 5].
Normal tissue dose limits should be respected (see below). Treatment planning essential to reduce dose to organs at risk; guidelines may be exceeded based on clinical circumstances [ESOPH-G 3 of 5].

Supportive care: treatment interruptions avoided; patients seen weekly with vital signs, weight, blood counts; prophylactic antiemetics; antacid/PPI/antidiarrheals as needed; enteral nutrition if caloric intake <1500 kcal/day [ESOPH-G 4 of 5].

dose_frameworks.name: Preoperative RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients receiving preoperative chemoradiation for resectable disease [ESOPH-G 4 of 5]

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For definitive chemoradiation in nonsurgical candidates or for those who decline surgery; also for patients at risk for not having surgery due to comorbidities [ESOPH-G 4 of 5]

dose_frameworks.name: Postoperative RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients with R1/R2 resection or node-positive adenocarcinoma after suboptimal surgery [ESOPH-G 4 of 5]

approaches.name: Preoperative chemoradiation (preferred for SCC; category 1 for both SCC and selected adenocarcinoma)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Preferred: paclitaxel/carboplatin (CROSS trial), fluorouracil/oxaliplatin (FOLFOX). Other: fluorouracil/cisplatin (category 1), irinotecan/cisplatin (category 2B), paclitaxel/fluoropyrimidine (category 2B) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]
approaches.indication: Resectable T2–T4a, N0–N+ esophageal or EGJ cancer; preferred for SCC [ESOPH-G]
approaches.key_trial: CROSS trial [167]: median OS 49 months vs. 24 months (surgery alone); HR 0.657; pCR 49% SCC, 23% adenocarcinoma. NEOCRTEC5010 [322] for SCC: median OS 100.1 vs. 66.5 months; HR 0.71.
approaches.toxicities: Esophagitis, fatigue, hematologic (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), nausea, pneumonitis, cardiotoxicity

approaches.name: Definitive chemoradiation
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Preferred: paclitaxel/carboplatin, FOLFOX (category 1), fluorouracil/cisplatin (category 1). Other: cisplatin/docetaxel, cisplatin/paclitaxel, irinotecan/cisplatin (category 2B), paclitaxel/fluoropyrimidine (category 2B) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]
approaches.indication: Unresectable T4b, non-surgical candidates, or patients who decline surgery with locoregional disease [ESOPH-G]
approaches.key_trial: RTOG 85-01 [260,335]: chemoradiation vs. RT alone: median OS 14 vs. 9 months; 5-year OS 27% vs. 0%. PRODIGE5/ACCORD17 [298]: FOLFOX vs. fluorouracil/cisplatin: median PFS 9.7 vs. 9.4 months, not statistically different.
approaches.toxicities: Acute: esophagitis, nausea, vomiting, mucositis, hematologic, fatigue. Late: esophageal stricture, cardiac toxicity, lung fibrosis.

approaches.name: Postoperative chemoradiation (fluoropyrimidine-based)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Fluorouracil-based (e.g., bolus fluorouracil/leucovorin per INT-0116, but modified doses preferred due to toxicity) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]
approaches.indication: Adenocarcinoma: R1/R2 resection, or node-positive with suboptimal surgery/poor nodal harvest, or understaged at diagnosis [ESOPH-17]. SCC: R1/R2 resection [ESOPH-6].
approaches.key_trial: INT-0116 [295,296]: median OS 36 vs. 27 months (surgery alone); 3-year OS 50% vs. 41%; 10-year follow-up confirmed benefit.
approaches.toxicities: Hematologic (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), GI (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea), fatigue, anastomotic stricture, cardiac toxicity

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: Variable: e.g., 30 Gy in 10 fractions, 20 Gy in 5 fractions; may use short course
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not routinely; may use chemoradiation for symptom control in selected patients (e.g., TROG 03.01 trial [460] for dysphagia)
approaches.indication: Palliation of dysphagia, pain, bleeding from advanced/unresectable tumors [ESOPH-H]
approaches.key_trial: TROG 03.01 [460]: chemoradiation vs. RT alone for dysphagia palliation: no significant difference in dysphagia relief (45% vs. 35%, p=0.13), but higher toxicity with chemoradiation.
approaches.toxicities: Esophagitis, fatigue, fibrosis, fistula risk (especially with brachytherapy or high doses)

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used in several settings: perioperative (neoadjuvant/adjuvant) for resectable disease, as part of definitive chemoradiation, and as palliative treatment for advanced/metastatic disease. Regimens recommended for advanced esophageal adenocarcinoma, EGJ adenocarcinoma, and gastric adenocarcinoma may be used interchangeably except as indicated. Selection is based on performance status, comorbidities, toxicity profile, histology (SCC vs. adenocarcinoma), and biomarkers (HER2, PD-L1, MSI/MMR, CLDN18.2, NTRK, BRAF, RET). Oxaliplatin is preferred over cisplatin due to lower toxicity. Two-drug cytotoxic regimens are preferred for advanced disease; three-drug regimens reserved for medically fit patients with good PS. Trastuzumab is added to first-line chemotherapy for HER2-positive adenocarcinoma (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+). Checkpoint inhibitors should be added to first-line chemotherapy for patients with PD-L1 CPS ≥1. CPS and TAP scores are highly concordant and may be interchangeable [ESOPH-F 1 of 12]. An FDA-approved biosimilar is appropriate for any recommended biologic therapy. Subcutaneous formulations of pembrolizumab (berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph) and nivolumab (hyaluronidase-nvhy) may be substituted for IV with different dosing instructions [ESOPH-F 1 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: FLOT (fluorouracil, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, docetaxel) – category 1 [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]; FLOT + durvalumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 or TAP ≥1% – category 1 for EGJ adenocarcinoma, category 2A for esophageal adenocarcinoma; category 2B for CPS <1 or diffuse type EGJ adenocarcinoma [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: FLOT4 trial [168] showed improved median OS (50 vs. 35 months; HR 0.77) compared to ECF. FLOT + durvalumab (MATTERHORN) [3] improved EFS; diffuse type showed no OS advantage.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other recommended: Fluorouracil + cisplatin (category 1), Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin (category 2A). Useful in certain circumstances for MSI-H/dMMR: neoadjuvant immunotherapy (dostarlimab, nivolumab+ipilimumab, pembrolizumab, tremelimumab+durvalumab) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Definitive chemoradiation (locally advanced, not surgical candidate or declining surgery)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Paclitaxel + carboplatin (category 1) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]; Fluorouracil + oxaliplatin (category 1) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CROSS trial [167] established paclitaxel/carboplatin. PRODIGE5/ACCORD17 [298] showed FOLFOX noninferior to fluorouracil/cisplatin.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluorouracil + cisplatin (category 1), Cisplatin + docetaxel or paclitaxel, Irinotecan + cisplatin (category 2B), Paclitaxel + fluoropyrimidine (category 2B) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Paclitaxel + carboplatin (category 1) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]; Fluorouracil + oxaliplatin (category 1) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CROSS trial [167] for both SCC and adenocarcinoma. FOLFOX shows comparable efficacy.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluorouracil + cisplatin (category 1), Irinotecan + cisplatin (category 2B), Paclitaxel + fluoropyrimidine (category 2B) [ESOPH-F 3 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Postoperative (after preoperative chemoradiation and R0 resection with residual disease – both SCC and adenocarcinoma)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate-577 [294]: median DFS 22.4 vs. 11 months (HR 0.69) for nivolumab vs. placebo in patients with residual disease after preoperative chemoradiation.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Observation if not candidate for further therapy. For adenocarcinoma who received perioperative chemotherapy, continue systemic therapy (category 1). For R1/R2 resection and no prior RT, fluoropyrimidine-based chemoradiation [ESOPH-F 3 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for unresectable locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic adenocarcinoma – HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + trastuzumab (category 2A) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + trastuzumab + pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + cisplatin + trastuzumab (category 1) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + cisplatin + trastuzumab + pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ToGA trial [156]: trastuzumab + chemo improved OS (13.8 vs. 11 months). KEYNOTE-811 [347]: adding pembrolizumab to trastuzumab + chemo improved ORR (74% vs. 52%). Oxaliplatin preferred over cisplatin for lower toxicity.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other combinations of fluoropyrimidine + platinum + trastuzumab may be considered [ESOPH-F 4 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for unresectable locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic adenocarcinoma – HER2-negative
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + nivolumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + zolbetuximab-clzb for CLDN18.2 positive (category 1 for EGJ, category 2A for esophageal) [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin alone [ESOPH-F 4 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate-649 [348]: nivolumab + chemo improved OS (14.4 vs. 11.1 months) for CPS ≥5. KEYNOTE-859 [37] and KEYNOTE-590 [36] support pembrolizumab. SPOTLIGHT [39] and GLOW [40] support zolbetuximab for CLDN18.2+. RATIONALE-305 [38] supports tislelizumab.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluoropyrimidine + cisplatin + pembrolizumab/tislelizumab (category 1 for CPS ≥5); Fluoropyrimidine + cisplatin; FOLFIRI; Paclitaxel ± carboplatin/cisplatin; Docetaxel ± cisplatin; Fluoropyrimidine alone; DCF modifications [ESOPH-F 4 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for unresectable locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic SCC
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + nivolumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 5 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 5 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 5 of 12]; Oxaliplatin + paclitaxel + tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 5 of 12]; Fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin; Nivolumab + ipilimumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 5 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate-648 [350]: nivolumab + chemo improved OS (15 vs. 9 months) and nivolumab + ipilimumab also improved OS in PD-L1 ≥1%. KEYNOTE-590 [36] and RATIONALE-306 [68] support pembrolizumab and tislelizumab. RATIONALE-306 also supports oxaliplatin/paclitaxel/tislelizumab.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluoropyrimidine + cisplatin + nivolumab/pembrolizumab/tislelizumab (category 1 for CPS ≥1); Cisplatin + paclitaxel + tislelizumab; Fluoropyrimidine + cisplatin; FOLFIRI; Paclitaxel ± carboplatin/cisplatin; Docetaxel ± cisplatin; Fluoropyrimidine alone; DCF [ESOPH-F 5 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-line or subsequent therapy for adenocarcinoma
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ramucirumab + paclitaxel (category 1 for EGJ, category 2A for esophageal) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]; Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki for HER2-positive (category 1) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]; Docetaxel (category 1) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]; Paclitaxel (category 1) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]; Irinotecan (category 1) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]; Fluorouracil + irinotecan (FOLFIRI) (category 1) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]; Trifluridine + tipiracil for third-line or subsequent EGJ adenocarcinoma (category 1) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: RAINBOW [384]: ramucirumab + paclitaxel improved OS (9.6 vs. 7.4 months). DESTINY-Gastric01 [385]: fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan improved OS vs. chemo (12.5 vs. 8.4 months). COUGAR-02 [343]: docetaxel vs. active symptom control improved OS (5.2 vs. 3.6 months). TAGS [395]: trifluridine/tipiracil improved OS (5.7 vs. 3.6 months) vs. placebo.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ramucirumab alone (category 1 for EGJ, category 2A for esophageal) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12]; Irinotecan + cisplatin; FOLFIRI + ramucirumab; Irinotecan + ramucirumab; Docetaxel + irinotecan (category 2B). Useful in certain circumstances: entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib for NTRK fusions, pembrolizumab (MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H), nivolumab+ipilimumab (MSI-H/dMMR), dostarlimab (MSI-H/dMMR), dabrafenib+trametinib (BRAF V600E), selpercatinib (RET fusion) [ESOPH-F 6 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-line or subsequent therapy for SCC
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Nivolumab (category 1) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12]; Pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥10 (category 1) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12]; Docetaxel (category 1) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12]; Paclitaxel (category 1) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12]; Irinotecan (category 1) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12]; Tislelizumab-jsgr (category 1) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12]; Fluorouracil + irinotecan (FOLFIRI) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ATTRACTION-3 [386]: nivolumab improved OS vs. chemo (10.9 vs. 8.4 months). KEYNOTE-181 [387]: pembrolizumab improved OS in CPS ≥10 (9.3 vs. 6.7 months). RATIONALE-302 [94]: tislelizumab improved OS vs. chemo.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Irinotecan + cisplatin; Docetaxel + irinotecan (category 2B). Useful in certain circumstances: entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib for NTRK fusions, pembrolizumab (MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H), nivolumab+ipilimumab (MSI-H/dMMR), dostarlimab (MSI-H/dMMR), dabrafenib+trametinib (BRAF V600E), selpercatinib (RET fusion) [ESOPH-F 7 of 12].

key_regimens.name: FLOT
key_regimens.setting: Perioperative (neoadjuvant and adjuvant) for resectable adenocarcinoma
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 14 days; 4 cycles pre- and 4 cycles postoperatively
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, infection, fatigue, neuropathy, stomatitis, diarrhea, nausea

key_regimens.name: Paclitaxel + Carboplatin (CROSS regimen)
key_regimens.setting: Preoperative or definitive chemoradiation (SCC and adenocarcinoma)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Weekly for 6 weeks concurrent with RT (41.4-50.4 Gy in 23-28 fractions)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, fatigue, esophagitis, neuropathy, hypersensitivity

key_regimens.name: Fluoropyrimidine + Oxaliplatin + Nivolumab (CheckMate-649-like)
key_regimens.setting: First-line advanced adenocarcinoma (PD-L1 CPS ≥5) or SCC (CPS ≥1)

drugs.day: Day 1 every 2 weeks (FOLFOX) or 130 mg/m2 every 3 weeks (CAPOX)

key_regimens.name: Trastuzumab + Fluoropyrimidine + Platinum (ToGA-like)

drugs.name: Fluorouracil

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Cardiotoxicity (LVEF monitoring), neutropenia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fatigue. Alternative: oxaliplatin-based combination.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (CheckMate-648-like)
