treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment After Preoperative or Definitive Therapy
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Assessment should be performed ≥5 to 8 weeks after completion of preoperative therapy and prior to surgery. For definitive chemoradiation, assessment endoscopy with biopsy may be deferred to 6 or more weeks after completion [ESOPH-A 6 of 8]. For patients undergoing non-operative management (e.g., immunotherapy), surveillance imaging and EGD should be every 12 weeks for 2 years, then every 6 months until year 5 [ESOPH-A 6 of 8, ESOPH-13A].
response_logic: After preoperative chemoradiation: FDG-PET/CT and chest/abdomen CT with contrast (not required if FDG-PET/CT done) are recommended. EGD with biopsy is optional if surgery is planned, but recommended if surgery is not being considered [ESOPH-5, ESOPH-14, ESOPH-15]. In SCC: if no evidence of disease → esophagectomy or observation (category 2B); if persistent local disease → esophagectomy (preferred) or palliative management; if unresectable/metastatic → palliative management [ESOPH-5]. In adenocarcinoma (post preoperative chemoradiation): same logic; observation category 2B for no evidence of disease [ESOPH-15].

After perioperative systemic therapy (adenocarcinoma): FDG-PET/CT and chest/abdomen CT with contrast (not required if FDG-PET/CT done) and EGD with biopsy. If no evidence of disease and patient declines surgery or has unresectable disease → definitive chemoradiation; if medically unfit → definitive chemoradiation or palliative management; if metastatic → palliative management [ESOPH-14].

After neoadjuvant immunotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR tumors: FDG-PET/CT, chest/abdomen CT, EGD with biopsy (EUS as clinically indicated). If no evidence of disease → observation or esophagectomy; if persistent local disease → esophagectomy (preferred) or palliative management; if new metastatic → palliative management [ESOPH-16]. Note: current imaging and endoscopic methods do not reliably predict pCR; esophagectomy remains standard outside prospective trials [ESOPH-13A].

After definitive chemoradiation: FDG-PET/CT, chest/abdomen CT, and EGD with biopsy. If no evidence of disease → observation (preferred) or esophagectomy; if persistent local disease → esophagectomy (preferred) or palliative management; if new metastatic → palliative management [ESOPH-5, ESOPH-15].

Post-treatment FDG-PET results should not be used to select patients for surgery since FDG-PET cannot distinguish microscopic residual disease [ESOPH-5A, MS-11]. FDG-PET is predictive of pathologic response and survival but has limited utility early after therapy due to radiation-induced inflammation [MS-10-11].
imaging_recommendations: FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or FDG-PET scan from skull base to mid-thigh, performed ≥5 to 8 weeks after completion of preoperative therapy [ESOPH-5A, ESOPH-14, ESOPH-15, ESOPH-16].

Chest/abdomen CT with oral and IV contrast (not required if FDG-PET/CT is done). Pelvis CT if clinically indicated. CT with contrast should be considered if pre-treatment CT findings merit more accurate anatomic assessment (e.g., small pulmonary nodules) or if post-treatment PET identified new findings [ESOPH-5A, ESOPH-14, ESOPH-15A, ESOPH-16].

EGD with biopsy: optional if surgery is planned (to confirm absence of residual disease or provide tissue if non-operative management). Recommended if surgery is not being considered [ESOPH-5A, ESOPH-14, ESOPH-15A].
EUS exams after chemotherapy or RT have reduced ability to accurately determine present stage; biopsies may also not accurately diagnose residual disease [ESOPH-A 6 of 8].

For non-operative management of adenocarcinoma with MSI-H/dMMR after neoadjuvant ICI: chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (+PET/CT if clinically indicated) and EGD with biopsy (EUS as clinically indicated) every 12 weeks for 2 years, then every 6 months until year 5 [ESOPH-13A, ESOPH-16].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy of suspicious findings on imaging or endoscopy is recommended. Strictures should be biopsied to rule out neoplastic cause. EUS-guided FNA should be performed if suspicious lymph nodes or wall thickening seen on cross-sectional imaging [ESOPH-A 6 of 8].

If persistent local disease after preoperative chemoradiation: esophagectomy is preferred for both SCC and adenocarcinoma. For SCC, observation is category 2B if no evidence of disease; for adenocarcinoma, observation is category 2B [ESOPH-5, ESOPH-15].
If persistent local disease after definitive chemoradiation: salvage esophagectomy is preferred if resectable and medically fit. Otherwise, palliative management [ESOPH-5, ESOPH-15].
If new metastatic disease after any primary treatment: proceed to palliative management (systemic therapy and/or best supportive care) [ESOPH-5, ESOPH-14, ESOPH-15, ESOPH-16].
For patients who are nonsurgical candidates and have persistent local disease after definitive chemoradiation, consider palliative RT or best supportive care [ESOPH-8, ESOPH-19].

prognosis.overall: The prognosis for esophageal and EGJ cancers is generally poor, with an estimated 5-year survival rate remaining low in the United States (Siegel et al., 2023) [8]. Globally, esophageal cancer is the sixth leading cause of cancer-related deaths, with wide geographic variation in incidence [2,3]. Survival is strongly influenced by histologic type, pathologic stage, and treatment response. In the CROSS trial, preoperative chemoradiation plus surgery yielded a 5-year overall survival (OS) of 47% compared to 34% with surgery alone for resectable disease (T2-3,N0-1,M0) [167,310]. The NEOCRTEC5010 trial in esophageal SCC showed median OS of 100.1 months with preoperative chemoradiation versus 66.5 months with surgery alone [322]. For locally advanced adenocarcinoma, perioperative FLOT chemotherapy achieved median OS of 50 months versus 35 months with ECF [168]. The MAGIC trial established a 5-year OS benefit of ~13% for perioperative chemotherapy over surgery alone (36% vs 23%) [332]. Nivolumab adjuvant therapy after preoperative chemoradiation for residual disease improved median disease-free survival from 11 to 22.4 months (CheckMate 577) [294].

by_stage.stage: Clinical stage I (T1,N0) - SCC or adenocarcinoma
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly provided in source; treatment with endoscopic therapy or esophagectomy offers high cure rates (low risk of nodal metastases) [20,207].
by_stage.context: Early-stage disease includes pTis, pT1a, and select pT1b. Endoscopic therapy is preferred for low-risk tumors (≤2 cm, well/moderately differentiated, no LVI) with low recurrence risk [20,244,245].

by_stage.stage: Clinical stage II-III (locoregional) - trimodality therapy (preop chemoradiation + surgery)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: CROSS trial: 5-year OS 47% (preop chemoradiation + surgery) vs 34% (surgery alone) [310]. For SCC subgroup, median OS 81.6 vs 21.1 months; for adenocarcinoma subgroup, median OS 43.2 vs 27.1 months [310].
by_stage.context: Preoperative chemoradiation (paclitaxel/carboplatin) followed by esophagectomy is a standard approach for medically fit patients with resectable disease [167,310].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: FLOT regimen: median OS 50 months vs 35 months (ECF); 5-year OS not explicitly stated but hazard ratio 0.77 (95% CI 0.63-0.94) [168]. MAGIC trial: 5-year OS 36% vs 23% with perioperative ECF [332].
by_stage.context: Perioperative chemotherapy is a category 1 option for adenocarcinoma of esophagus or EGJ, particularly FLOT or fluoropyrimidine/oxaliplatin [168,292].

by_stage.stage: yp stage 0 (pathologic complete response after neoadjuvant therapy)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: No specific numbers given; but patients with yp stage 0 have lower relapse rates than those with residual disease. The CheckMate 577 trial showed benefit of adjuvant nivolumab for yp T+ and/or N+ disease [294].
by_stage.context: Pathologic complete response (pCR) after preoperative chemoradiation is associated with improved survival [100-106]. Residual primary tumor is associated with shorter OS [101,107].

by_stage.stage: Unresectable locally advanced or metastatic disease
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Poor; median OS with first-line chemotherapy + immunotherapy is approximately 12-14 months in selected populations (PD-L1 CPS ≥5) [348,349]. ATTRACTION-3: nivolumab second-line for SCC gave median OS 10.9 vs 8.4 months [386].
by_stage.context: Palliative systemic therapy can prolong survival and improve quality of life. Performance status guides treatment intensity. Targeted therapies (HER2, PD-L1, CLDN18.2) are used in biomarker-selected populations [156,348,349].

prognostic_factors: Histologic type: SCC has worse survival than adenocarcinoma in the AJCC 8th edition dataset [10,47].
Pathologic stage: depth of invasion (pT), nodal status (pN), and distant metastases (pM) are strongly prognostic [46,47].
Histologic grade: higher grade (G3-4) associated with worse survival [46].
Age: advancing age is associated with decreased survival [46].
Tumor location: more distal location within the esophagus is associated with better survival [46,47].
Treatment response: pathologic complete response after neoadjuvant therapy is a favorable prognostic factor [100-106].
Resection margin status: R0 resection is associated with better outcomes; R1/R2 with worse [GG].
Lymphovascular invasion (LVI) and poor differentiation are poor prognostic features in early-stage disease treated endoscopically [kk, aaa].
PD-L1 expression, HER2 status, MSI-H/dMMR, and CLDN18.2 expression guide systemic therapy and are prognostic in advanced disease [c].
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis in esophageal and EGJ cancers is stage-dependent and heavily influenced by histology and biology. In the US and Western Europe, adenocarcinoma predominates and is often diagnosed at advanced stages due to lack of screening. Nearly 50% of patients have disease beyond locoregional confines at diagnosis, contributing to low survival rates. Multimodality therapy (preoperative chemoradiation or perioperative chemotherapy) has improved outcomes for locoregional disease. The incorporation of immune checkpoint inhibitors and targeted agents offers additional benefit in advanced disease for biomarker-selected patients. Long-term survivors remain at risk for late recurrences, second primary cancers, and treatment-related toxicities.

disease_related.management: Assess severity with standardized scale (Grade 0-4) [454]. For non-curative patients, consider esophageal stent placement (self-expanding metal stents), but avoid in surgical candidates or during chemoradiation due to risk of adverse events [9,10,11]. External beam radiotherapy (EBRT), brachytherapy, photodynamic therapy, and chemotherapy are also options [1,15]. Wire-guided dilation or balloon dilation for severe obstruction, with caution for perforation [7].

Endoscopic lumen restoration (retrograde/antegrade). If not feasible, establish enteral access (J-tube or gastrostomy). EBRT, brachytherapy (if lumen restored), PDT, chemotherapy. Surgery in select cases. For severe obstruction (liquids only): stent placement (partially or fully covered), EBRT, brachytherapy [1,15].

Acute bleeding may be from aorto-esophageal fistula – endoscopic intervention risky, may lead to exsanguination. Tumor surface bleeding: endoscopic electrocoagulation (bipolar, argon plasma) – may initially control but recurrence rate is high [16]. Chronic blood loss: EBRT [16].
Treat per NCCN Antiemesis guidelines. Evaluate for luminal obstruction; if present, consider endoscopic or fluoroscopic luminal enhancement.
Assess and treat tumor-related pain per NCCN Adult Cancer Pain guidelines. Severe uncontrolled pain after stent placement: consider endoscopic stent removal.
Risk increased with dilation of malignant strictures; use wire-guided techniques and avoid overdilation. Management is supportive and may require surgical intervention.
Stent placement may be used, but with caution. Large-diameter stents may increase risk of bleeding and fistula [13].

chemotherapy: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia) – common with docetaxel, carboplatin, irinotecan. G-CSF may be used for primary prophylaxis if indicated [ESOPH-F 1]. Note: the guideline does not specify G-CSF dosing schedules but mentions two-drug regimens have lower toxicity.
Peripheral neuropathy – oxaliplatin, docetaxel; consider duloxetine for painful neuropathy (ineffective for numbness/tingling) [ESOPH-J 2].
Nausea/vomiting – managed with antiemetics (prophylaxis with 5-HT3 antagonists, NK1 antagonists as per NCCN Antiemesis) [ESOPH-H].
Fatigue – common; encourage physical activity and energy conservation; assess contributing factors [ESOPH-J 2].
Anorexia and weight loss – nutritional counseling, enteral feeding if needed [ESOPH-H].
Hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine, fluorouracil) – dose modifications, supportive care.
Nephrotoxicity (cisplatin) – hydration, avoid if renal impairment; oxaliplatin preferred over cisplatin to reduce toxicity [ESOPH-F].
immunotherapy: Checkpoint inhibitor-related toxicities: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, adrenal insufficiency), nephritis, dermatologic reactions. Management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [ii, b].
Immune-mediated adverse events occur with nivolumab, pembrolizumab, ipilimumab, durvalumab, tislelizumab, dostarlimab. Grade 3-4 events occur in 13-47% of patients in trials [294,348,350].
Interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis reported with fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (12 patients, 1 death) [385].
targeted_therapy: Trastuzumab: infusion reactions, cardiotoxicity (not well described in esophageal context but known from breast cancer).
Ramucirumab: hypertension (16% vs 8% in REGARD), neutropenia when combined with paclitaxel [383,384].
Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki: neutropenia (51% grade ≥3), anemia (38%), pneumonitis/interstitial lung disease [385].
Zolbetuximab: nausea, vomiting, infusion-related reactions (from GLOW/SPOTLIGHT) [12,13].
surgery: Anastomotic leak – cervical anastomosis may have less severe consequences [201].
Anastomotic stricture – dilation may be needed.
Reflux symptoms – due to loss of lower esophageal sphincter; manage with proton pump inhibitors, bile acid binders, and lifestyle modifications (avoid lying flat, use wedge pillow) [ESOPH-J].

Delayed gastric emptying – affects up to 37% of patients; encourage small frequent meals (5/day), low fat/fiber. Consider referral for botulinum toxin injection if pyloric emptying procedure not done [ESOPH-J].
Dumping syndrome – frequent meals, high protein/fiber, low simple carbohydrates, avoid fluids with meals [ESOPH-J].
Malnutrition and weight loss – progressive weight loss expected in first 6 months; monitor vitamin B12, folic acid, vitamin D, calcium; consider dietician referral [ESOPH-J].
Pulmonary complications – especially with open transthoracic approaches; minimally invasive esophagectomy may reduce risk [192,193].
radiation: Esophagitis – acute; managed with supportive care, nutritional support, treatment breaks if needed but avoid [ESOPH-G 4].
Pneumonitis – lung dose constraints (V20Gy ≤33%, mean <18 Gy) help reduce risk [ESOPH-G 3].
Cardiotoxicity – increased risk of cardiac death after RT; monitor cardiac function, coordinate with PCP for risk factor management [17-20, ESOPH-J 2].
Fistula formation – rare but serious, especially with brachytherapy boost [266].
Late effects: pericarditis, myocardial infarction, chronic pulmonary fibrosis, secondary malignancies [ESOPH-J].

supportive_care.overview: Best supportive care is essential from diagnosis through all stages, regardless of curative or palliative intent. The goal is to prevent and relieve suffering and support the best possible quality of life. A multimodality interdisciplinary approach is encouraged, including surgical oncology, medical oncology, gastroenterology, radiation oncology, radiology, pathology, nutrition services, social workers, nurses, and palliative care specialists [ESOPH-H, ESOPH-E]. For patients with advanced disease, palliative/best supportive care may be combined with systemic therapy depending on performance status.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional assessment and counseling are recommended for all patients at diagnosis [ESOPH-1]. Enteral feeding tubes (preferably jejunostomy) should be considered for preoperative nutritional support in patients with significant dysphagia or weight loss. Percutaneous gastrostomy may be used for cervical esophageal tumors receiving definitive chemoradiation or marginally resectable disease, but must be discussed with surgeon prior to placement to avoid compromising gastric conduit [ESOPH-2, ESOPH-11]. During chemoradiation, if caloric intake <1500 kcal/day, oral and/or enteral nutrition should be considered [ESOPH-G 4]. Feeding jejunostomy or nasogastric tubes may be placed as needed. For palliative dysphagia, enteral access (J-tube or gastrostomy) is indicated if endoscopic luminal restoration is not possible [ESOPH-H]. In survivorship, monitor weight regularly, especially in first 6 months; monitor vitamin B12, folic acid, vitamin D, calcium levels; refer to dietician for individualized counseling [ESOPH-J]. Encourage small frequent meals for delayed gastric emptying and dumping syndrome.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Prophylactic antiemetics should be given when appropriate during radiation therapy and chemotherapy [ESOPH-G 4]. For nausea/vomiting, treatment should follow NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis [ESOPH-H]. Nausea and vomiting may also be due to luminal obstruction, so endoscopic/fluoroscopic evaluation is warranted [ESOPH-H]. Specific antiemetic regimens (e.g., 5-HT3 antagonists, NK1 antagonists) are not detailed in this guideline but are referenced to the separate NCCN Antiemesis guideline.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: The guideline does not explicitly discuss G-CSF prophylaxis or management. It notes that two-drug cytotoxic regimens are preferred for lower toxicity and that FLOT has significant myelosuppression but no specific G-CSF recommendations. For febrile neutropenia prevention, clinicians should refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Myeloid Growth Factors, which are not reproduced here.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: VTE prophylaxis is not specifically addressed in the extracted guideline sections. The panel recommends standard oncologic VTE risk assessment and prophylaxis per institutional practice.
supportive_care.pain_management: Tumor-related pain should be assessed and treated in accordance with the NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain [ESOPH-H]. Severe uncontrolled pain following esophageal stent placement should prompt endoscopic removal of the stent [ESOPH-H]. For chemotherapy-induced neuropathy, duloxetine is recommended for painful neuropathy (not effective for numbness or tingling) [ESOPH-J].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Assess for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [ESOPH-1, ESOPH-1A]. Social workers, nurses, and other support disciplines are desirable participants in multidisciplinary team meetings [ESOPH-E]. Smoking cessation advice, counseling, and pharmacotherapy are recommended as indicated [ESOPH-1].
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental care is not specifically addressed in the extracted sections. However, given the use of radiation therapy that may involve the esophagus and the potential for mucositis, basic oral care is implied. No specific dental prophylaxis is mentioned.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up after curative treatment should be systematic but remains controversial with no high-level evidence for optimal surveillance algorithms [ESOPH-I]. Stage-specific surveillance is based on retrospective data and panel consensus. For patients with pStage 0–I (Tis, T1a, T1b) treated endoscopically, endoscopic surveillance (EGD) is recommended at intervals based on depth of invasion: Tis/T1a: every 3 months year 1, every 6 months year 2, then annually indefinitely [ESOPH-A 6, ESOPH-I Table 1]. T1b (N0 on EUS): EGD every 3 months year 1, every 4-6 months year 2, then annually; imaging (CT chest/abdomen) every 6 months for 2 years then annually to 5 years may be considered [ESOPH-I Table 1]. For stage II–III treated with definitive chemoradiation, EGD every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually for 3 years; imaging every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually to 5 years [ESOPH-I]. For trimodality therapy (preop chemoradiation + surgery), imaging (CT chest/abdomen) every 6 months for 2 years, then annually to 5 years; EGD as clinically indicated [ESOPH-I]. For esophagectomy alone (± adjuvant), imaging every 6 months for 2 years then annually to 5 years; EGD as needed based on symptoms [ESOPH-I]. General follow-up for asymptomatic patients: H&P every 3-6 months for 1-2 years, every 6-12 months for 3-5 years; chemistry and CBC as clinically indicated; imaging and EGD as clinically indicated [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20]. Nutritional assessment and counseling should continue [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The rationale for surveillance is based on the pattern of recurrence: ~90% of relapses occur within the first 2 years after completion of local therapy, but potentially actionable relapses can occur >5 years later [ESOPH-I]. Locoregional relapse is common after definitive chemoradiation (especially SCC), making EGD valuable [3]. After trimodality therapy, locoregional relapse is less common, so imaging is preferred. Surveillance beyond 5 years is generally not recommended for esophageal/EGJ-specific cancer, but annual H&P may detect second primary cancers [ESOPH-J]. The goal is to detect early recurrence amenable to salvage therapy, though high-level evidence of survival benefit from intensive surveillance is lacking [ESOPH-I].
late_effects_screening: Malnutrition/malabsorption: Monitor weight regularly; progressive weight loss expected first 6 months. Consider monitoring vitamin B12, folic acid, vitamin D, calcium levels. Refer to dietician [ESOPH-J].

Delayed gastric emptying: Encourage small frequent meals (5/day), minimize high fat/fiber. Refer to gastroenterology for refractory symptoms; consider botulinum toxin injection of pylorus if emptying procedure not done [ESOPH-J].
Dumping syndrome: Frequent meals, high protein/fiber, low simple carbohydrates, avoid fluids with meals [ESOPH-J].
Reflux symptoms: Avoid lying flat after eating, use wedge pillow; consider PPI (though usually biliary reflux) [ESOPH-J].
Anastomotic stricture: Evaluate for and treat with dilation if symptomatic dysphagia [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20].
Radiation-induced cardiotoxicity: Coordinate with PCP for cardiac risk factor management (hypertension, diabetes, lipids). Consider referral to cardiologist as clinically indicated [ESOPH-J].
Chemotherapy-induced neuropathy: Assess for painful neuropathy; duloxetine for pain (not for numbness/tingling) [ESOPH-J].
Fatigue: Encourage physical activity, energy conservation; address contributing medical/psychosocial factors [ESOPH-J].
Second primary cancers: Annual H&P; consider screening for head and neck cancers in SCC patients due to risk of second primary SCC [ESOPH-J].
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: For SCC, locoregional recurrence is common after definitive chemoradiation (up to 47% at 5 years in historical series) [260,335]. After trimodality therapy, locoregional recurrence is less common; most recurrences are distant [308,437]. For adenocarcinoma, recurrence patterns depend on stage and treatment. yp stage III patients have higher relapse rates and earlier recurrences compared to yp stage 0 patients. The CROSS trial showed that preoperative chemoradiation reduced locoregional recurrence from 34% to 14% and peritoneal carcinomatosis from 14% to 4% [308]. After perioperative chemotherapy, locoregional and distant recurrences occur. Recurrences within the first 2-3 years are most frequent, but late recurrences (>5 years) are possible. Salvage strategies for locoregional recurrence include esophagectomy (if resectable and medically fit) or chemoradiation/chemotherapy/palliative care [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: For Tis/T1a treated with ER/ablation: EGD every 3 months for year 1, every 6 months for year 2, then annually indefinitely [ESOPH-I Table 1].
For T1b (N0 on EUS) treated with ER/ablation: EGD every 3 months year 1, every 4-6 months year 2, then annually indefinitely; EUS may be considered with EGD [ESOPH-I Table 1].

For T1b or greater, any N treated with esophagectomy ± adjuvant: Imaging (CT chest/abdomen with IV contrast) every 6 months for first 2 years, then annually for up to 5 years; EGD as needed based on symptoms and imaging findings [ESOPH-I Table 1].
For any T, any N treated with neoadjuvant therapy + esophagectomy: Imaging every 6 months for up to 2 years, then annually for up to 5 years; EGD as clinically indicated [ESOPH-I Table 1].

For T1b–T4, N0–N+, T4b treated with definitive chemoradiation: Imaging every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then annually for up to 5 years; EGD every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then annually for 3 more years [ESOPH-I Table 1].
For asymptomatic patients after curative therapy: H&P every 3-6 months for 1-2 years, then every 6-12 months for 3-5 years [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20].
imaging_strategy: CT chest/abdomen with oral and IV contrast (unless contraindicated) is preferred for surveillance imaging [ESOPH-I].
Alternative imaging: PET/CT or MRI as clinically indicated if patient cannot undergo CT [ESOPH-I footnote b].
FDG-PET/CT is useful for response assessment after chemoradiation (≥5-8 weeks after completion) but not for routine surveillance in all settings [ESOPH-5A, ESOPH-14, ESOPH-15].

CT with contrast should be considered if pre-treatment CT findings merit more accurate anatomic assessment (e.g., small pulmonary nodules) or if post-treatment PET identifies new findings [dd, ESOPH-5A, ESOPH-15A].
Pelvis CT with contrast as clinically indicated for distal lesions [cc].
No imaging surveillance is recommended for Tis or T1a tumors after endoscopic therapy [ESOPH-I Table 1].
laboratory_monitoring: Chemistry profile and CBC as clinically indicated during follow-up [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20].
Monitor vitamin B12, folic acid, vitamin D, and calcium levels in post-esophagectomy patients due to risk of deficiencies [ESOPH-J].
Monitor blood glucose and blood pressure after esophagectomy as weight loss may improve hypertension and hyperglycemia [ESOPH-J].
No routine tumor marker surveillance is recommended.
supportive_follow_up: Nutritional assessment and counseling at regular intervals [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20].
Dilatation of anastomotic or treatment-induced strictures as needed [ESOPH-9, ESOPH-20].
Assess for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer [ESOPH-1].
Counsel on healthy lifestyle: maintain healthy weight, physical activity, healthy diet, limit alcohol, smoking cessation [ESOPH-J].
Coordinate with PCP for routine health maintenance and cancer screening (breast, colorectal, prostate, lung for average-risk survivors) [ESOPH-J].

special_situations.key_message: Perioperative immunotherapy should be considered in consultation with a multidisciplinary team. Current imaging and endoscopic assessment do not reliably predict pathologic complete response, so esophagectomy remains standard outside clinical trials.
special_situations.management: For patients with MSI-H/dMMR esophageal or EGJ adenocarcinoma: consider neoadjuvant immune checkpoint inhibitor (e.g., dostarlimab-gxly, nivolumab+ipilimumab, pembrolizumab, tremelimumab+durvalumab) [ESOPH-F 3]. If non-operative management is pursued, immunotherapy should be given for at least 1 year (perioperative + adjuvant), and surveillance should include chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (+PET/CT if indicated) and EGD with biopsy (EUS as indicated) every 12 weeks for 2 years, then every 6 months until year 5 [ESOPH-13A, ESOPH-16 footnote uu].
special_situations.evidence: Cercek A, et al. N Engl J Med 2025;392:2297-2308; Raimondi A, et al. J Clin Oncol 2025;43:3457-3467 [37,38].

special_situations.key_message: Trastuzumab should be added to first-line chemotherapy for HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+ and ISH/FISH+) adenocarcinoma. Pembrolizumab may also be added for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 with cisplatin-based or oxaliplatin-based chemo).
special_situations.management: First-line: fluoropyrimidine (fluorouracil or capecitabine) + oxaliplatin (preferred over cisplatin) + trastuzumab ± pembrolizumab (if CPS ≥1). Alternative: cisplatin-based regimen + trastuzumab ± pembrolizumab. For second-line: fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (category 1) after prior trastuzumab [ESOPH-F 4, ESOPH-F 6].
special_situations.evidence: ToGA trial (Bang et al, Lancet 2010) [34]; KEYNOTE-811 (Janjigian et al, Nature 2021) [32,33]; DESTINY-Gastric01 (Shitara et al, N Engl J Med 2020) [70].

special_situations.key_message: Zolbetuximab in combination with fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin is a first-line option for CLDN18.2-positive (≥75% viable tumor cells with moderate-strong membrane staining) HER2-negative tumors.
special_situations.management: First-line: fluoropyrimidine (fluorouracil or capecitabine) + oxaliplatin + zolbetuximab-clzb (category 1 for EGJ adenocarcinoma; category 2A for esophageal adenocarcinoma) [ESOPH-F 4]. CLDN18.2 testing by IHC is required [ESOPH-B 4].
special_situations.evidence: GLOW trial (Shah et al, Nat Med 2023) [12]; SPOTLIGHT trial (Shitara et al, Lancet 2023) [13].

special_situations.situation: NTRK gene fusion-positive tumors (adenocarcinoma or SCC)
special_situations.key_message: TRK inhibitors (entrectinib, larotrectinib, repotrectinib) are useful in certain circumstances for NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion-positive tumors in second-line or subsequent therapy.
special_situations.management: Test for NTRK fusions using validated assays (IHC/ISH/NGS). If positive, entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib (for post-TRK progression) are options [ESOPH-F 6, ESOPH-F 7]. Category 2B for first-line? Actually not listed as first-line; only in second-line/subs. The regimen was removed from first-line Useful in Certain Circumstances in version 1.2026 [Updates].
special_situations.evidence: Drilon et al, N Engl J Med 2018 [86]; Doebele et al, Lancet Oncol 2020 [85]; Solomon et al, Ann Oncol 2023 [87].

special_situations.situation: T4b (unresectable) tumor invading trachea, great vessels, or heart
special_situations.key_message: These tumors are considered unresectable. Treatment options include definitive chemoradiation (if able to tolerate) or palliative chemotherapy alone in the setting of invasion of trachea, great vessels, or heart. For select patients, endoluminal stenting may be appropriate for palliation.
special_situations.management: SCC: definitive chemoradiation or consider chemotherapy alone. Adenocarcinoma: definitive chemoradiation (only if decline surgery) or chemotherapy alone. Palliative management (ESOPH-10, ESOPH-21). For dysphagia palliation, consider endoscopic stenting [ESOPH-H].
special_situations.evidence: Guideline pathway: ESOPH-4 (SCC), ESOPH-13 (adenocarcinoma). Endoluminal stenting per ESOPH-H.

special_situations.situation: Cervical esophageal cancer (≤5 cm from cricopharyngeus)
special_situations.key_message: Should be treated with definitive chemoradiation rather than surgery due to high morbidity of laryngopharyngectomy.
special_situations.management: Definitive chemoradiation [ESOPH-C 1]. Feeding tube (gastrostomy) may be considered for nutritional support during chemoradiation [ESOPH-2, ESOPH-11].

special_situations.situation: Nonsurgical candidates with early-stage disease (Tis-T1b, N0)
special_situations.key_message: Endoscopic therapy (ER ± ablation) is the primary treatment option. Definitive chemoradiation may be offered for tumors with poor prognostic features (LVI, poor differentiation, submucosal invasion ≥200 μm for SCC or >500 μm for adenocarcinoma, positive margins, or size ≥2 cm).
special_situations.management: For pTis/pT1a: ER ± ablation or ablation alone. For pT1b,N0: ER ± ablation (or definitive chemoradiation if poor features). For cT1b-T4a,N0-N+: definitive chemoradiation if able to tolerate; if not, palliative RT or best supportive care [ESOPH-8, ESOPH-19].
special_situations.evidence: ESOPH-8, ESOPH-19; references Draganov PV et al. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol 2019 [kk, aaa].

special_situations.situation: Positive peritoneal cytology without visible implants (M1 disease)
special_situations.key_message: Positive peritoneal cytology is defined as M1 disease and is associated with poor prognosis. Consider laparoscopic staging with peritoneal washings for patients with clinical T3 or N+ tumors (especially Siewert II/III).
special_situations.management: Patients with positive peritoneal cytology are considered metastatic and should be managed with systemic therapy and palliative care [ESOPH-C 1].

clinical_pearls: Multidisciplinary team management is essential before initiating any therapy for localized esophagogastric cancer. Joint review of staging studies (CT, EUS, PET/CT, endoscopy) by surgical, medical, and radiation oncology, radiology, and pathology optimizes treatment planning [ESOPH-E].

Endoscopic resection (ER) is critical for accurate staging of early-stage cancers (Tis, T1a, T1b ≤2 cm) because it provides more precise depth of invasion than EUS. ER can be both diagnostic and therapeutic for low-risk tumors (≤2 cm, well-differentiated, no LVI, negative margins) [ESOPH-A].

For locally advanced adenocarcinoma of the esophagus or EGJ, perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT or FOLFOX) and preoperative chemoradiation (CROSS regimen) have shown comparable survival in the NEO-AEGIS trial, making both valid options. FLOT is preferred for medically fit patients with good PS; preoperative chemoradiation is preferred for borderline resectable tumors or when FLOT is not feasible [ESOPH-F 1].

CheckMate 577 established that adjuvant nivolumab for 1 year improves disease-free survival in patients with residual disease after preoperative chemoradiation and R0 resection, regardless of PD-L1 expression. This is now a category 1, preferred option for yp T+ and/or N+ [294].

PD-L1 testing (CPS or TAP score) is recommended for all newly diagnosed patients, and a checkpoint inhibitor should be added to first-line chemotherapy for advanced disease with PD-L1 CPS ≥1. CPS and TAP scores are highly concordant and interchangeable [ESOPH-B 5, ESOPH-F 1].

For early-stage SCC (pTis, pT1a), endoscopic therapy is preferred. Ablation of SCC after ER has low evidence, but can be used for multifocal HGD. Ensure all remaining dysplasia is eradicated. For high-risk T1b SCC (≥200 μm invasion, LVI, poor differentiation), esophagectomy or chemoradiation is recommended due to risk of nodal metastases [ESOPH-A 2, ESOPH-A 5].

Surveillance after definitive chemoradiation for locally advanced disease should include EGD every 3-6 months for 2 years then annually for 3 years, plus imaging every 3-6 months for 2 years then annually to 5 years, because locoregional recurrence is common [ESOPH-I Table 1].

Patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors have high response rates to immune checkpoint inhibitors. For resectable disease, non-operative management is investigational; esophagectomy remains standard outside clinical trials. If non-operative management is chosen, immunotherapy should be continued for at least 1 year with intensive surveillance [ESOPH-13A].

In advanced esophageal cancer, two-drug cytotoxic regimens (e.g., FOLFOX or XELOX) are preferred over three-drug regimens due to lower toxicity. Oxaliplatin is preferred over cisplatin. Trastuzumab is added for HER2-positive disease; zolbetuximab for CLDN18.2-positive; checkpoint inhibitors for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 [ESOPH-F].

Minimally invasive esophagectomy (MIE) approaches (Ivor Lewis or McKeown) are strongly considered due to improved postoperative outcomes compared to open surgery, including lower pulmonary complications and shorter hospital stay. Robotic-assisted MIE is emerging but requires further study [ESOPH-C 2].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Esophageal and Esophagogastric Junction Cancers
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 3.2026. Covers staging, workup, treatment, surveillance, and survivorship for SCC and adenocarcinoma. Category of evidence and consensus ratings. Updated annually.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides TNM classification and stage groupings for esophageal and EGJ cancers including clinical, pathologic, and post-neoadjuvant (yp) staging. Includes Siewert classification for EGJ adenocarcinoma [10].

guidelines_resources.title: ASGE guideline on screening and surveillance of Barrett's esophagus
guidelines_resources.notes: Published 2019. Suggests screening strategy for BE based on family history and GERD risk factors [ESOPH-A 1].

guidelines_resources.title: AGA Clinical Practice Update on New Technology and Innovation for Surveillance and Screening in Barrett's Esophagus
guidelines_resources.notes: Published 2022. Suggests screening with standard upper endoscopy in individuals with ≥3 risk factors for BE and EAC. Nonendoscopic cell-collection devices may be considered [ESOPH-A 1].

guidelines_resources.title: Diagnosis and Management of Barrett's Esophagus: An Updated ACG Guideline
guidelines_resources.notes: Published 2022. Recommends single screening endoscopy in patients with chronic GERD and ≥3 additional risk factors. Swallowable capsule device is acceptable alternative [ESOPH-A 1].

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the Examination of Specimens from Patients with Carcinoma of the Esophagus
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides standardized reporting templates for esophageal and EGJ cancer specimens. Includes tumor regression scoring (modified Ryan scheme) [109, ESOPH-B 2].

guidelines_resources.title: Endoscopic Submucosal Dissection for Superficial Gastrointestinal Lesions Guideline - Update 2022
guidelines_resources.notes: Cited in Principles of Endoscopic Staging and Therapy [11, ESOPH-A 7].

key_trials.full_name: Chemoradiotherapy for Oesophageal Cancer Followed by Surgery Study
key_trials.population: Resectable esophageal or EGJ cancer (T2-3,N0-1,M0); 75% adenocarcinoma, 23% SCC
key_trials.intervention: Preoperative chemoradiation (paclitaxel 50 mg/m2 and carboplatin AUC2 weekly) + surgery
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 49 months (intervention) vs 24 months (control); HR 0.657 (95% CI 0.495-0.871; p=0.003). R0 resection rate 92% vs 69% (p<0.001). 1-,2-,3-,5-year OS: 82%,67%,58%,47% vs 70%,50%,44%,34% [167,310].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Locoregional recurrence reduced from 34% to 14% (p<0.001); peritoneal carcinomatosis from 14% to 4% (p<0.001). pCR rate 49% for SCC, 23% for adenocarcinoma [308].
key_trials.practice_change: Established preoperative chemoradiation with paclitaxel/carboplatin as standard of care for resectable esophageal cancer. Category 1 recommendation.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Nivolumab in Resected Esophageal or Gastroesophageal Junction Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with completely resected (R0) esophageal or EGJ cancer with residual pathologic disease after preoperative chemoradiation
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 240 mg IV every 2 weeks for 16 weeks, then 480 mg every 4 weeks for up to 1 year
key_trials.key_result: Median DFS 22.4 months (nivolumab) vs 11.0 months (placebo); HR 0.69 (95% CI 0.56-0.85; p<0.001) [294].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: DFS benefit independent of PD-L1 expression. Grade 3-4 adverse events 13% vs 6%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant nivolumab as standard for patients with residual disease after neoadjuvant chemoradiation and R0 resection. Category 1, preferred.

key_trials.full_name: First-Line Nivolumab Plus Chemotherapy Versus Chemotherapy Alone for Advanced Gastric, Gastro-Oesophageal Junction, and Oesophageal Adenocarcinoma
key_trials.population: Previously untreated, HER2-negative, unresectable advanced or metastatic gastric, EGJ, or esophageal adenocarcinoma
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 360 mg every 3 weeks + capecitabine/oxaliplatin or modified FOLFOX
key_trials.primary_endpoint: OS and PFS in patients with PD-L1 CPS ≥5
key_trials.key_result: OS (CPS ≥5): median 14.4 vs 11.1 months; HR 0.71 (p<0.0001). PFS (CPS ≥5): 7.7 vs 6.0 months; HR 0.68 (p<0.0001). Benefit also seen in CPS ≥1 and all randomized [348].
key_trials.practice_change: Established nivolumab + chemotherapy as first-line standard for advanced gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma with PD-L1 CPS ≥5 (category 1) and as a preferred option for CPS ≥1.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab Plus Chemotherapy Versus Chemotherapy Alone for First-Line Treatment of Advanced Oesophageal Cancer
key_trials.population: Previously untreated, locally advanced or metastatic esophageal cancer (SCC, adenocarcinoma, or EGJ adenocarcinoma)
key_trials.intervention: Pembrolizumab 200 mg every 3 weeks + cisplatin + fluorouracil (or capecitabine+oxaliplatin allowed later?)
key_trials.primary_endpoint: OS and PFS in patients with SCC and PD-L1 CPS ≥10, SCC, all patients
key_trials.key_result: OS in SCC CPS ≥10: 13.9 vs 8.8 months; HR 0.57 (p<0.0001). OS in SCC: 12.6 vs 9.8 months; HR 0.72 (p=0.0006). OS in all patients: 12.4 vs 9.8 months; HR 0.73 (p<0.0001). PFS in SCC: 6.3 vs 5.8 months; HR 0.65 (p<0.0001) [349].
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab + chemotherapy as first-line treatment for esophageal cancer, particularly for SCC and PD-L1 CPS ≥10. Category 1 for combination with cisplatin; preferred option.

key_trials.full_name: Perioperative Chemotherapy with Fluorouracil Plus Leucovorin, Oxaliplatin, and Docetaxel Versus Fluorouracil or Capecitabine Plus Cisplatin and Epirubicin for Locally Advanced, Resectable Gastric or Gastro-Oesophageal Junction Adenocarcinoma
key_trials.population: Resectable gastric or EGJ adenocarcinoma (≥cT2 and/or N+)
key_trials.intervention: Perioperative FLOT (4 cycles pre and 4 cycles post)
key_trials.comparator: Perioperative ECF (3 cycles pre and 3 cycles post)
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 50 months (FLOT) vs 35 months (ECF); HR 0.77 (95% CI 0.63-0.94). pCR rate 16% vs 6% (p=0.02) [168].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 adverse events 25% vs 40% (FLOT lower). Serious adverse events 27% in both groups.
key_trials.practice_change: FLOT became the preferred perioperative chemotherapy regimen for resectable gastric/EGJ adenocarcinoma, replacing ECF. Category 1 recommendation.

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab in Combination With Chemotherapy Versus Chemotherapy Alone for Treatment of HER2-Positive Advanced Gastric or Gastro-Oesophageal Junction Cancer
key_trials.population: HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or FISH+) advanced gastric or EGJ adenocarcinoma
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 13.8 vs 11.0 months; HR 0.74 (95% CI 0.60-0.91; p=0.046). In IHC 2+/FISH+ or IHC 3+ subgroup: median OS 16.0 vs 11.8 months; HR 0.65 [156].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 47% vs 35%; grade 3-4 adverse events similar.
key_trials.practice_change: Established trastuzumab + chemotherapy as first-line standard for HER2-positive advanced gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab Versus Chemotherapy in Patients with Advanced Oesophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma Refractory or Intolerant to Previous Chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Advanced esophageal SCC refractory or intolerant to at least one prior fluoropyrimidine- and platinum-based regimen
key_trials.comparator: Investigator's choice of docetaxel or paclitaxel
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 10.9 vs 8.4 months; HR 0.77 (95% CI 0.62-0.96; p=0.019). OS benefit observed regardless of PD-L1 expression [386].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 19.3% vs 21.5%; median duration of response 6.9 vs 3.9 months. Grade 3-4 adverse events 18% vs 63%.
key_trials.practice_change: Nivolumab became a category 1 preferred second-line option for advanced esophageal SCC.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab Versus Chemotherapy as Second-Line Therapy for Advanced Esophageal Cancer
key_trials.population: Advanced SCC or adenocarcinoma of esophagus/EGJ progressing after first-line therapy
key_trials.comparator: Investigator's choice of docetaxel, paclitaxel, or irinotecan
key_trials.primary_endpoint: OS in SCC PD-L1 CPS ≥10, SCC, and all patients
key_trials.key_result: OS in SCC CPS ≥10: 9.3 vs 6.7 months; HR 0.69 (95% CI 0.52-0.93; p=0.007). 12-month OS 43% vs 20%. No significant OS benefit in overall population or SCC overall [387, 92].
key_trials.practice_change: Pembrolizumab became a category 1 preferred second-line option for esophageal SCC with PD-L1 CPS ≥10.

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant Chemoradiotherapy Followed by Surgery Versus Surgery Alone for Locally Advanced Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Esophagus
key_trials.intervention: Preoperative chemoradiation (vinorelbine + cisplatin with RT) + surgery
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 100.1 vs 66.5 months; HR 0.71 (95% CI 0.53-0.96; p=0.025). 5-year OS rates not explicitly given. R0 resection rate 98.4% vs 91.2% (p=0.002). Median DFS 100.1 vs 41.7 months; HR 0.58 (p<0.001) [322].
key_trials.practice_change: Strengthened evidence for preoperative chemoradiation in SCC, showing long-term survival benefit over surgery alone.
